gmaps_reviews_top3: Peter Kanji(1): My wife, with acquired neurodivergence related to PTSD, trusted London Psychiatry Clinic to document her ongoing harms during an extremely sensitive and distressing time when she was experiencing severe symptoms and repeatedly requested documentation. Unfortunately, not only did the clinic completely fail to document these harms, but the doctor also made no notes during the assessment. The assessment letter provided did not meet professional standards and is evidence of the doctor’s complete lack of engagement with my wife’s situation and a total dismissal of the factors contributing to her harm. To make matters worse, the doctor misframed my wife’s legitimate concerns about ongoing harm and harassment by applying discriminatory labeling to her communication style, which is a natural protective response related to her acquired neurodivergence. This perpetuated the very harm she was urgently requesting to be documented. Such mischaracterisation by a healthcare professional not only undermines trust but also constitutes a breach of professional duty and standards of care. The clinic’s failure to maintain contemporaneous clinical notes perpetuated significant harm,  exacerbating her PTSD symptoms and contributing to premature perimenopausal health complications.  PTSD is a serious condition that profoundly affects the neuroendocrine system, altering key hormones such as oxytocin and vasopressin and disrupting the regulation of stress hormones like cortisol. These disruptions can significantly impact physical health, including contributing to premature perimenopausal complications. The clinic’s failure to maintain contemporaneous clinical notes caused significant harm to my wife, who has acquired neurodivergence related to PTSD. PTSD is a serious condition that disrupts hormonal and neurological balance, affecting stress regulation and overall health. However, the doctor disregarded all this and treated my wife’s situation based on his own assumptions and misunderstandings of PTSD rather than accurately documenting what she reported during her assessment. This reveals a profound lack of knowledge and understanding of the condition, leading to substandard care. Despite repeated requests for accurate documentation and reasonable adjustments, her needs remained unmet. The clinic not only failed to provide an acceptable assessment letter but also falsely justified minimal recording as standard practice. This justification is misleading and directly violates professional standards, especially given my wife’s urgent need for accurate documentation. The doctor made no notes, showed zero engagement with my wife’s concerns, and instead provided an irrelevant and inappropriate rant about ChatGPT, which the clinic absurdly calls “standard practice.” Such failures amount to a breach of duty and fall far below the expected standards of care. I advise those seeking mental health care to avoid clinics that lack transparency and rely on misleading language to exploit distressing situations and patient vulnerability. Transparency and honesty are essential for trust and effective care. Based on our experience, this clinic lacks honesty and transparency, using misleading language to justify grave breaches of their duty of care. Such practices obscure the truth and exploit vulnerable patients at their most distressing moments. | I. Harrison(5): We felt clearly that our 10 year old adopted daughter needed help. After thorough research we found the London Psychiatry Clinic Website. It was very helpful to read all the information and watch the videos. It is so important to find somebody with whom you can be totally yourself, open and trusting. The first contact with the London Psychiatry Clinic was very encouraging. They have always been friendly and efficient with emails. We consulted Dr Hayley Ponsford, who is very experienced, a fantastic listener and happy to go the extra mile. We now feel confident that our daughter will receive the help she needs. | john bickle(1): Don't waste your time you can't just call to get an appointment takes days e-mailing  back and forth only 1 decent shrink works there and always fully booked the rest are far from nice Bonkers how they  are allowed to do an "assessment  over the phone" avoid this place is my advice

gmaps_reviews_top3: TOM ALO WM(1): My experience with Resources for Autism has unfortunately been extremely disappointing and frustrating. From the outset, the service has felt chaotic and poorly managed.  Firstly, the communication system is unnecessarily confusing. Having two separate phone numbers for London and Birmingham makes little sense. A single phone line with clear menu options would be far more effective. When I called, the staff member I spoke to seemed uninterested, unwilling to answer questions, and simply tried to pass me on. Staff should be trained and equipped with knowledge about both branches so that they can provide consistent, accurate information. Instead, I was left feeling dismissed and unsupported.  When I raised my concerns, I was told to email my details so someone could call me back. I waited over a month with no response. Only after I chased it up did anyone acknowledge my email. Even then, I was told that I shouldn’t have been promised a call in the first place—something I found very unprofessional. If a service promises to follow up, it should follow through. Ignoring emails—especially when they outline personal struggles and support needs—is unacceptable. A simple acknowledgment message or short response would have been far better than silence.  The overall handling of my case has been poor: •	Promises were made that were not kept. •	My email was ignored until I chased it. •	Staff appeared untrained, disinterested, and inconsistent in their information. •	Communication across the organisation seemed disjointed, from reception to admin to management.  I had real hopes that Resources for Autism could provide meaningful support. Instead, I felt let down, fobbed off, and left questioning whether an organisation that cannot manage basic communication is truly autism-friendly.  What should have happened is simple: someone should have explained what services are available, clarified the referral process, and set realistic expectations. That would have gone a long way to building trust. Instead, the lack of professionalism and broken promises have completely undermined my confidence in the service.  I strongly urge Resources for Autism to reflect on: •	Communication processes (including phone lines and email responses). •	Staff training, ensuring all staff can provide clear and consistent information. •	Transparency about services offered, without overpromising or backtracking. •	Building an approach that is genuinely autism-friendly, which means clarity, consistency, and reliability.  At present, my experience has been so negative that I would not recommend Resources for Autism. Unless these serious issues are addressed, others will likely feel the same disappointment and frustration that I did. | Michelle(1): I reached out in february, because I felt the signs of an approaching autistic burnout. I was hoping to learn any copying mechanisms, or at least receive some sort of support / someone to talk to without judgement, which is according to their website something they take pride in.  I had a phone call with someone who said they'll put me on the waiting list for therapy, and they'll send me some helpful information.  The information provided was unfortunately hardly of any use, and I don't think they put me on the waiting list at all. They told me they'd get back to me in October for therapy. It is December as I am writing this and I still haven't heard back from them, even after reaching out per email last month...  In the end, I did burn out, lost my job, and now struggle to find a new one, which is exactly what I wanted to avoid by reaching out, so thanks for nothing! | Sa ku Ra(5): Their staff are all well trained and friendly.  My son had a great experience with them.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Mike Hutchison(5): I went with ADHD due to the good reviews, there are a lot of negative reviews but they all generally looked from a year ago and more. The more recent reviews seem positive so I went for it. Compared to other places which definitely were ‘dodgy’ I plumbed for the ADHD centre. The first and initial assessment was booked quickly and within weeks. I was diagnosed with ‘Inattentive ADHD’ and went down the medication route, at 56 I didn’t want to waste time. It’s not cheap, but getting my ADHD attended to was more important than money. I’m in the titration process and so far so good and I’ve had no issues with psychiatrist or the office staff. I see so many bad reviews that ADHD centre never got back to them or emailed back, why don’t these people just not pick up the phone. If you’re not happy with the psychiatrist they gave you, ask for another it’s not difficult… anyway, ADHD sucks! | Rebekah Tune(1): Edit: having published this review I have had contact from the clinic who have to be fair been very apologetic and on request have refunded my money. I feel like my complaint was taken seriously and hope future assessments by the doctor I saw are much improved.  I have been so distressed by my ADHD assessment that I don’t know what to do. The Dr was rude and dismissive and as others have said, just racing through a tick box exercise impatiently, snapping at me “is it a yes or a no?”. The whole process was traumatic and I feel worse than ever. I’m not even willing to finish the process with them now, I feel completely helpless and like they just took my money and left me hanging in a terrible state. | Joshua Felice Scimeca(1): I’ve had a truly awful experience dealing with this company. My “doctor” was late for every appointment, sometimes I was waiting on zoom for 20 minutes staring at a blank screen. Once the doctor arrived and was in the process of giving me life changing information, he appeared to be solely interested in box ticking without expanding on the clearly scripted questions or showing any concern for his patient in need. Consultations were over in 10 minutes (after having waited 20 minutes for the doctor) at a cost to me of about £20/minute. Sadly, this was just beginning.  I was started on the lowest possible doses of two medications, not the recommended doses. At first this seems like a responsible thing to do. However, each consultation is close to £200 plus the prescription from their private pharmacy was well over £100, totalling hundreds of pounds per consultation. The increments that they decided to increase my medication with each consultation were laughably small leading to many consultations. In hindsight, I have come to realise that at this point they were milking me for all the money they could get.  By the time I noticed the medication working (possibly placebo). I’d spent thousands, this left me with the decision to move forward with the treatment or have huge sunk costs. As a man desperately trying to improve his life, I decided to move forward only to find more costs and more pain.  Throughout the treatment process and on their website they speak of a “shared care agreement” between them and the GP which means no more consultations and 1/10th the cost of the prescribed medication via the NHS. On their site they ask you to discuss this agreement with your GP before embarking on the process, which I did. However, I could not be specific because I did not know the exact protocol which was to be recommended. I tried to get this sorted as soon as possible. After weeks of waiting for documents along with paying hundreds for the medication through the ADHD centre’s private pharmacy (another money spinner). I came to find that my GP does not in fact support this type of agreement at all, rarely do any doctors according to my GP. In my opinion, doctors do not do this type of thing as it increases their liabilities and they want to shield themselves from third party intervention as much as possible.  As one of the prescriptions is a medicine used regularly by the NHS, my GP could provide that one alone. Desperate, worn down from decades of pain for as long as I can remember and thousands of pounds down. I tried it the single medication. It was awful. I had constant sleepless nights, I do not wish to go in to further detail on a public page but just know that it was an extremely difficult time. When they contacted me guess what ? They wanted me to pay hundreds more for another consultation because I’d “stopped taking one of the medications”. I have spent thousands with this firm and I’m left with nothing. They prey on the mental weaknesses of the general public in order to generate profit. In my opinion, the dregs of society. I wish I could leave a 0 star review.

They went above and beyond to enhance my job advertisement, helping me attract the ideal candidate to join my company. Picture-IT Ltd. | Amanda Boyd(5): Since the  beginning, I have referred many people to Exceptional individuals . Their help has been invaluable for all ages. Interesting podcasts, innovative events , good advice! Important to be able to identify with other neurodivergent individuals on a non biased platform. | S J(1): Dear Exceptional Individuals Team,  I am writing to express my deep disappointment following a recent experience with your organisation. My outreach was not for myself, but on behalf of a neurodiverse family member who is currently seeking employment and in need of support. I sought out a company like yours specifically because of your stated commitment to inclusion and support, but the experience we had was anything but supportive.  After uploading her CV to your website, we received no confirmation — not even an automated email. Concerned, we attempted to follow up by phone, only to be told via message that, due to high call volumes, you are unable to respond to all calls. While this was understandable, we were then advised to email instead, with the assurance that a team member would get back to us.  However, after sending the email, we received a reply stating that the company does not respond to emails either. At that point, it became clear that there was no viable way to communicate with your team at all. This complete lack of accessibility — being told not to call and then told you won’t respond to emails — left us both confused and frustrated.  Given the overwhelmingly positive reviews we had seen, our experience was shockingly inconsistent and left us feeling misled. Both my relative and I feel thoroughly let down. As such, we request that all of our personal data be removed from your website immediately.  This has been a total waste of time and a deeply disappointing experience, especially considering the vulnerability of those you claim to support. I sincerely hope your internal processes are reviewed to prevent others from going through the same.

We've been sitting in this train for over 4 minutes, not moving, no announcements. One thing is for sure, if you need to be somewhere on time, do not rely on TfL. You might lose your job over it, seriously! | Filip I.(5): Transport for London (TfL): A Commuter's Lifeline  Rating: ★★★★★  Transport for London (TfL) is the backbone of London's transportation system and a true lifeline for commuters and residents alike. As a frequent user of TfL services, I can confidently say that it is one of the best public transportation networks in the world, offering a seamless and efficient travel experience.  One of the most commendable aspects of TfL is its extensive coverage. The network spans across the entire Greater London area, providing comprehensive connectivity through its buses, Underground (Tube), Overground, DLR (Docklands Light Railway), Trams, and River services. No matter where you need to go within the city, TfL has you covered, ensuring that you can reach your destination conveniently and reliably.  TfL's commitment to customer satisfaction is evident in the quality and frequency of its services. The Underground, in particular, operates with remarkable efficiency, with trains arriving at regular intervals, even during peak hours. The buses are a reliable mode of transportation, with extensive routes and accessibility features, making them suitable for people of all abilities. The Overground, DLR, Trams, and River services seamlessly integrate with other modes of transport, offering alternative and scenic travel options.  TfL's dedication to passenger comfort is noteworthy. Stations and vehicles are well-maintained and clean, ensuring a pleasant travel experience. The accessibility features, including step-free access, priority seating, and assistance for passengers with disabilities, demonstrate TfL's commitment to inclusivity and ensuring that everyone can travel with ease.  The Oyster card and contactless payment system introduced by TfL have revolutionized ticketing and made travel more convenient. The pay-as-you-go system, along with the daily and weekly fare caps, ensures that passengers are charged fairly, with no need to worry about purchasing individual tickets for each journey. The TfL mobile app and website provide real-time travel updates, service disruptions, and journey planning tools, enabling passengers to stay informed and make informed decisions.  TfL's commitment to sustainability and reducing its environmental impact is commendable. The network continues to invest in greener transportation options, such as electric buses and energy-efficient trains, contributing to London's efforts to become a more sustainable and eco-friendly city.  Lastly, the customer service provided by TfL staff is exceptional. The employees are knowledgeable, helpful, and approachable, readily assisting passengers with inquiries, directions, and ensuring a safe and comfortable travel experience.  In summary, Transport for London (TfL) is a world-class public transportation network that plays a crucial role in keeping London moving. With its extensive coverage, efficient services, commitment to customer satisfaction, accessibility features, convenient ticketing options, sustainability initiatives, and outstanding customer service, TfL has truly set the benchmark for public transportation systems. Whether you're a daily commuter, a visitor to the city, or simply exploring London's vibrant neighborhoods, TfL is the reliable and convenient choice for getting around the capital. | K Mistry(1): Your 154 route is so unreliable! content:
NAS Enfield Branch (Enfield NAS - ENAS)Contact Address:Sophie Castell, Branch Officer,                 tel: 07827 081866, email: enfield@nas.org.uk
http://www.enfieldnas.org.uk
gmaps_formatted_address: 58 Melbourne Ave, London N13 4SX, UK
gmaps_reviews_top3: Mr. Puffin(1): I started going to ENAS groups after coming to terms with being autistic later in life, although I really didn’t have a good time here and ended up leaving in the end. The whole thing just felt to clinical and patronising to me and I found myself feeling judged and disrespected for being autistic anyway which made me uncomfortable.  I also didn’t seem to get on with the woman who runs these groups either even though she apparently has a child with autism, which I felt may have been a big part of her motivation for being involved with NAS in the first place. In terms of trying to understand her own child and work out how high functioning they were in comparison to other autistic people, or even to try and fix autistic people in some sense and make them more ‘normal’ in quite an ambitious and egotistical way. Which is completely the wrong attitude to have about autism anyway as far as I am concerned, since you can’t be something that you are not or pretend to be a horse when you are really a zebra.  I don’t know if this is reflective of NAS in general or if it is just a problem with the Enfield branch, but I could tell that many of the ideas and approaches that they had to autism here were not really accurate at all or even verging on delusional. So if this is the general outlook that NAS has about autism, then I just find it amazing that they have absolutely no understanding or insight into this sort of thing and are this ignorant. As I found it to be undermining and insulting rather than helpful, even after I tried to bring up these issues and improve communication.  To make matters even worse though was the fact that one of the other people who was working for ENAS and seemed to be very much involved with it was someone who used to actually make fun of me for being autistic or projected his own issues about it onto me and was very nice at all, and also doesn’t seem to have changed that much during this time. But apparently ENAS doesn’t seem to notice these things and be aware of such attitudes, and so could even be potentially enabling abusive situations to happen. Since I even ended up feeling worse in some ways through having joined when I was really hoping for something that would be more helpful.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Joseph Paice(1): This charity no longer take phone calls or provide a telephone service for people with autism ! They expect autistic people to be able to navigate and get whatever assistance they need on the internet. Completely defeats the object of what autism truly is and the struggles they have! Appalling service! I complained but they did nothing!  All I advise here is if you want to donate, consider Autism West Midlands, Autistica, Resources For Autism just to name a few. They all take phone calls and are ready to provide support and assistance there and then. Much better than this so called "charity" now! (If I could really call them a proper charity) | Alex Kowal(2): I have an Autistic nephew and wanted to raise money to help raise awareness of Autism for the charity and his special needs school. I advised them what I was doing and why 9 months in advance, what ever I raised was 50/50. To cut the long story short, they lost my application form and my materials request. Even though I asked for them over the phone after chasing to see where they are, still got the order wrong. They advertised my challenge once on social media 3 months before the challenge. Never got in touch to see how I'm progressing. After my challenge was finished, I got no well done or thank you. The only message I got was "I will no longer work for the charity in a few days, but if you need anything contact my colleague". For me raising money for them, they weren't prepared to help me and I had to do all the chasing. The special needs school did 10 times more. Such a shame I have to put a negative comment to a charity. If they see this then hopefully they'll book their ideas up to encourage more people like me to raise money for the family's that need help. | Lesley Jones(1): Over the last couple of years, I've urgently needed support relating to our high functioning son whomhas Asperger's.  An important turning point came when he grew so frustrated at having to live home, life became difficuklt as my husband has sight and memory problems, etc. my own health had a downturn then. I wrote and telephoned yourself, leaving messages and to a couple of your nationwide links. The last time early in 2022 a receptionist answered my call and I was told everyone was working from home.   There is no support at all for helping him find work, assisted living, etc.  He had saved up after working to stay in Florida for several weeks, incurring debts and now the round of frustrations has all started again.  BALANCE in Tolworth/Surbiton no longer exists. A worker at Kingston Carers was very helpful and suggested ideas some of which I'm on process of taking up.       A supervisor at my husband's memory clinic could see the difficulties we faced at home and suggested I contact yourselves.  She spoke very highly of how the Society could arrange good coinselling for our son, etc. etc.  What a hope!!

name: Ladbroke Grove Autism Centre
description_full:
At Ladbroke Grove autism centre we support people of all ages on the autism spectrum to help them achieve their full potential.
Social activities
We offer evening social groups for young people and adults on the autism spectrum, and a Saturday club for children aged five to 12 years old.
Evening social groups
Our social groups offer young people and adults the opportunity to develop social and communication skills, have fun, and meet other people on the autism spectrum. We organise a wide range of social events at the centre and in the community.
Saturday club
Our Saturday club for children aged 5 to 12 runs every week. The focus is on having fun while learning social and communication skills through sensory integration and play. Club membership operates on a rotating basis and each child has the opportunity to come for three months at a time.
For further information or to make a referral:
Tel: 0203 326 1200
Email: se.services@nas.org.uk
73C St Charles Square,London W10 6EJ

gmaps_reviews_top3: Suzanne Iwai(5): This is the Ladbroke Grove s branch of Assist. You can be diagnosed here for Autism and other life long conditions like Bi poplar, Personality Disorder,  and OCD after self referring or doing your own on line ADOS test. The lower floor of the building is devoted to a day centre that helps users with life skills like cooking (wish l could join them for lunch, akeays smells delicious) with recreation spaces for music, art and chat. Upstairs has counselling rooms, staff offices, and a sensory room. The building has just received a much needed update with double glazing throughout, a new intercom ( hurrah now someone buzzes you in )and some fresh paint. They also desperately need a new garden table n bench. The old one is looking dangerous with pieces charring off. I've fallen off it myself. description_full:
Our Mission is to support our clients to achieve their potential as individuals and to facilitate their social inclusion through employment.

There are a range of services including:
For Individuals:
Pre-employment support,
Workplace support and Coaching,
For Employers:
Recruitment, Consultancy, Management,
For Families, Individuals and Employers:
Various Workshops run throughout the year.
https://asmentoring.co.uk/what-we-do
Locations
The AS Mentoring team are based in 3 areas of the UK: London, Surrey and Birmingham.
We are very aware of the need for specialist support across the UK and further so we do work with clients remotely via Skype, phone-calls, email or a combination of all. If there is a need, staff can travel to other areas of the UK on an ad-hoc basis for Workplace Support and Coaching; this is something we'd be happy to discuss with you individually.

name: Care Trade
description_full:
http://www.care-trade.org/
Tel: 020 7060 5706
email: info@caretradeuk.org
CareTrade believes passionately that all young people should be given the opportunity to reach their potential and lead fulfilling lives. Just 15% of adults with autism are in employment – its our mission to change this. One of the ways we are tackling this issue is by offering support to help adults with autism gain the skills, confidence and experience they need to succeed in employment.
Answers for Autism Projects
REACH
CareTrade has a new employment project for people aged 25 years or over, who are on the autistic spectrum and who have been unemployed for 6 months or longer.
The Autism Project
This is a Full time 2 year employment programme for young people on the autism spectrum who would like work but need more confidence, support, skills and experience. We use a module based learning system so students can start at the beginning of any term and can progress at a pace that best meets their individual needs.
The School House Café
This is a 23 seat cafe set in a lovely Victorian building adjacent to Spa School. It provides high quality food and coffee to the community as well as work experience for young people with autism.

name: Ardeley- Work Experience and Family Visits at Church Farm
description_full:
Welcome to Church Farm
Open everyday at 9.00am. Parking is free. Buy a Farm Day Pass to explore the Woodland Play Area, indoor Little Farmers Room and our Feed the Animals Farm Trail. You can see 100's of Cattle, Sheep, Pigs and poultry and naturally grown fruit and vegetables.
Also based at the farm is Rural Care. We provide training and work experience for people with learning disabilities and/or mental health issues in a supportive and caring environment on a fully working farm. We also work in partnership with North Hertfordshire College to provide accredited training for their students with learning disabilities on the farm. Volunteers are welcome and can help out with Rural Care or can work on their own so they can enjoy the tranquility of the farm.
http://www.churchfarmardeley.co.uk/education-care/rural-care

gmaps_reviews_top3: James Matthews(5): I cant speak highly enough of this farm. I, along with some friends have camped here for the second year in a row, and we love it. The farm is so well kept with such detail on the experience of both the animals, and the public. The animals are clearly very well looked after, and the staff are very friendly and they also hire people with special needs to help with the animals, which just tops it off for me.  Will be coming back yearly! | Hannah Linder(5): What a great campsite. This was my first time camping and Church Farm Ardeley was a fantastic first experience! We loved everything about it from the large field we reserved so that our group could all camp together, to the fantastic farm and great outdoors that kept the kids entertained for days. The farm shop and cafe was amazing, with the shop stocking all the essentials and not at stupidly over inflated prices. All in all it was an amazing trip, I am a camping convert and I know we will back! | Lu Ke(3): We stayed her a week ago now camping over the weekend. The site was lovely and the farm was fun for the kids.  We went with my 1 and 4 year old kids, and picked it due to it claiming it was a quiet campsite.  However in the only field we could camp in, they had allowed two groups for twenty tents in, there were about 30 kids running around with water pistols until 11 at night. The large group acted like they owned the place, played music and sat up late loudly around a bonfire. Not what I would call a quiet campsite. On the first night I eventually got to sleep about 2 am.  In church farm's defence the security were great and did come out several times, but if did have to text them first. I felt for the guy trying to calm down such a large group.  Ultimately it made both nights stressful and made me not want to come back.  My advice to the site, split off the groups into a completely different field or make a designated quiet camping area for families with young kids.

name: Camden- Alexandra College
description_full:
Alexandra College
Ainsworth Way
London NW8 0SR
Alexandra College: No Limits Programme
Courses for Students with Learning Disabilities or Difficulties
Westminster Kingsway College offers provision for young people aged 16 to 25 with severe and complex needs that is delivered all year round in a newly refurbished centre in Camden. The new Alexandra College has been re-modelled to ensure that the learning and living space is fully accessible.
Curriculum
The No Limits Programme curriculum will focus on health, employment, independent living and community inclusion and the learning is underpinned by specialist support. Programmes will be flexible and individualised, focussing on what the students need to learn to support their transition from school to adulthood and prepare them for what they want to do next. As part of their Individual Learning Plan, students may access courses and vocational training opportunities at other Westminster Kingsway College Centres including catering, creative arts, health and beauty. Most importantly each learner will have a stimulating, meaningful and exciting time in college.
Entry requirements
Alexandra College is open to learners who are aged 16 to 25 years old (cannot be over 24 years old at the start of their final year at college), have a significant learning disability and may also have a physical disability.
Applications are invited from those who are unable to have their needs met by a school sixth form or general further education college programme for Learners with Learning Difficulties and Disabilities (LLDD) and have an Education, Health and Care Plan naming the provision (previously had a statement of educational needs or a learning disability assessment (LDA)).
This includes learners who:
-Have a severe learning disability or profound and multiple learning disabilities and may also have a physical disability
-May have an autism diagnosis
-Want to continue with their education
-Require a programme built around their individual needs
How to apply and further information:-
Places at Alexandra College must be agreed by your home borough (the borough in which you live) as the local authority funds places. Families cannot fund placements directly. Each young person for whom the local authority (in the borough where you live) agrees to consider a placement will be invited for an individual assessment to help decide if this is the right provision. To arrange this assessment, please contact the special educational needs (SEN) service in your borough.
For further information about Westminster Kingsway College’s provision for students with learning difficulties or disabilities, please contact the Programme Manager for Learners with Learning Difficulties and Disabilities at Westminster Kingsway College via 0870 060 9800 or email courseinfo@westking.ac.uk.

name: Crawley- A Potential Diamond
description_full:
We take the most natural approach for each individual. We get to know their families, communities and employers within easy reach of home. We focus on what positive attributes our jobseekers can bring to the workplace whilst embracing what adjustments the employer might need to make to live in harmony with the autism or learning disability.
Our model of employment support puts the emphasis on getting the job-fit right. Each one of our jobseekers will have their own unique set of circumstances that will make up the best job-fit. And we believe we do a very good job understanding exactly what these are.
Email:richard@apotentialdiamond.org
Phone:077389 41415.
Richard Lamplough has had, and continues to have, a varied career. For much of the last twenty years he's used his creativity, drive and communication skills to support people with autism, Asperger's Syndrome or mild learning disabilities into paid employment.

Aldenham Country Park
Aldenham Country Park consists of 100 acres of land surrounding a 60 acre lake. It has a farm with rare breed livestock such as Berkshire and British Lop pigs, Red Poll and Longhorn cattle, Black Welsh Mountain Sheep and free range poultry. All of this enables us at Rural Care to get involved in lots of different jobs and tasks.

The coolest thing is that there are teddy bears living on some of the enclosures! | Georgeta Buhnila(5): We visited end of October, the weather wasn’t great but we had a lot of fun. Elstree- Rural Care
Work experience 
 Aldenham Country Park, Rural Care provides training and work experience for people with learning disabilities and/or mental health issues in a supportive and caring environment on a fully working farm. We also work in partnership with North Hertfordshire College to provide accredited training for their students with learning disabilities on the farm. Volunteers are welcome and can help out with Rural Care or can work on their own so they can enjoy the tranquillity of the farm.

description_full:
Email: info@elbow-grease.co.uk
Telephone: 01279 445699
Mobile: 07725860794
http://www.elbow-grease.co.uk/
Elbow Grease Co Operative Ltd,
Circle Line House,
East Road,
Harlow
Essex,
CM20 2BJ
We currently have 26 adults with Learning Disabilities actively participating in the Elbow Grease Project. Their participation is typically on a sessional basis, the frequency chosen by them and dependent upon their commitment to their work role within the project.
The personal development of each member and the feedback from our customers, as to what our members bring to them, is far more than was ever anticipated.
Members volunteer and are supported to provide services to other vulnerable people living in their local community. They help with practical tasks in and around the home that the person is struggling to complete themselves. Common tasks include light housework, gardening, dog walking, shopping or simply befriending. The members have recently elected to do small jobs for local charities free of charge which brings a wealth of benefits. They also support other local voluntary groups in their promotional activities gaining from the social inclusion and friendships this brings.

When I got back in my car, I called the number and Liz confirmed I had spoken to her and she confirmed she did infact say it was OK for me to go there, she was so apologetic about how I was treated and said she'd investigate it. I got a call back an hour later from another lovely MOS apologising but she said the volunteer lady and the manager Allie denied any of the above happened. Said they were nothing but polite with me. http://www.greencorridor.org.uk/
Our Mission is to improve access for disadvantaged 14-25 year olds to sustained employment, education and training through practical environmental land based learning.
In our poly-tunnels and plots young people can dig, plant and grow, while our qualified tutors support them to achieve accreditations in horticulture and/or conservation. In our Learning and Development Centre young people are supported with regular 1:1’s, Employability qualifications/workshops and additional Maths and English qualifications, through our partners at the Nisai Group. Our small group sizes, of mixed abilities, promotes a conclusive environment of respect and trust, often encouraging social skills to blossom.

We are a supported employment services that supports people with a disability in to paid employment .
We believe with the right support, anyone can have a job. We run award-winning supported employment schemes right across Buckinghamshire.
We support clients to develop key skills which enable them to find paid employment. We do this via:
· Apprenticeship schemes (including chefs, cleaners and gardeners)
· A busy café
· A working farm which attracts thousands of visitors each month
· A garden centre in the picturesque Great Missenden
· A gardening and maintenance service, offering practical skills development
We have great links with many well-known companies too to help you find the right job.
The team overseeing this vital transition is called Back2Base.
Our teams will build confidence and when clients are ready to move on, they offer personal support to help you find job opportunities which are right for you and match any aspirations.

He wanted a medical consultant to be present so we could discuss changing my anti depressants and giving more insight into DPDR. The second (and last) time i went there i saw a completely different person. She made me go through all the same information again, even though she had thorough notes from my last session. She then went into another room to speak to the medical consultant. I was left in the room by myself for almost an hour. The medical consultant came in and appeared disinterested with my problems. It would seem that unless you’re on the verge of a complete mental breakdown, they’re not interested in helping you. name: Hillingdon- Rural Activities Garden Centre (RAGC) and the Rural Tea Rooms.
description_full:
The RAGC provides educational, supported employment and volunteering opportunities for residents with additional needs.
The RAGC produces a large range of bedding plants, shrubs and herbaceous plants, plus seasonal fruit, vegetables and free range eggs from chickens reared on site.
Also on the site and managed by Hillingdon Autistic Care & Support, are the Rural Tea Rooms. Here, they run the ‘Stepping Stones to Life skills and Employment’ project for young people affected by autism spectrum and learning disabilities. The aim of the project is to provide training and experience in a variety of areas which include Hospitality, Catering, Social Skills and Money Management Skills. All proceeds from the Rural Tea Rooms will be re-invested to support our learners.
http://www.hacs.org.uk/our-services/rural-tea-rooms/

About the Independent Lifestyles Service.
This is a support service for individuals with an Autistic Spectrum Condition (ASC) living in the community. It offers individualised support packages based upon the individuals needs. The service has been running for over 10 years.
The service operates across South East London, offering ASC specific, needs led, person centred support and:
- Is registered as a Personal Care Service with the Care Quality Commission
- Offers flexible, person centred support, seven days a week
- Offers support with daily skills such as budgeting, shopping, cooking and cleaning
- Helps to motivate individuals with personal care and developing self esteem
- Offers support to develop social and leisure activities and interests
- Offers support to access educational and work opportunities
- Supports individuals, with great success, in supported living accommodation. There has been staggering progress made by several of our supported living tenants.

How we support autistic people
Everyone has different likes and dislikes, needs and wishes. So, we put you at the centre of your support and build our support around you – not the other way round.
We will get to know you and to understand how you communicate and experience the world so that we can find out what support you need and what you want to do in your life. We will also get to know your routines, likes and dislikes so that we can help you to become more independent and live a fulfilling and happy life.
We can provide you with as much or as little support as you want, in many different ways such as:
-In your own home, in your family home or in shared accommodation.
-With your personal care, housework and finances.
-With employment, at college or university.
-Getting out and about.
-Going on holiday or pursuing your hobbies and pastimes.

Website: https://www.unitedresponse.org.uk

How we support autistic people

Everyone has different likes and dislikes, needs and wishes. So, we put you at the centre of your support and build our support around you – not the other way round.

We will get to know you and to understand how you communicate and experience the world so that we can find out what support you need and what you want to do in your life. We will also get to know your routines, likes and dislikes so that we can help you to become more independent and live a fulfilling and happy life. 

We can provide you with as much or as little support as you want, in many different ways such as:

-In your own home, in your family home or in shared accommodation.
-With your personal care, housework and finances.
-With employment, at college or university.
-Getting out and about.
-Going on holiday or pursuing your hobbies and pastimes.

About Ellingham Employment Services
Ellingham Employment Services Ltd. is a registered charity and company limited by guarantee, established in 1984.
We support people with disabilities within two main areas of speciality:
-Day Opportunities
-Employment Programmes
Ellingham are pioneering meaningful and empowering services for people with learning disabilities. Our I Can Ventures day opportunity service is a bold new vision. We want to do away with the mediocrity of traditional day services. Why should people with learning disabilities accept second best when choosing services?
We support individuals to learn and develop vocational and life skills, whilst enjoying social interaction and the chance to participate in fun, positive and constructive activities.
Employment Programmes
We work with people with a wide range of disabilities that may make it difficult for them to find employment and run programmes designed to assist them into work. In addition we provide advice and support to employers to help them employ and retain people with disabilities.
-Work Choice
-Work Programme
-Work Smart
-Access to Work
We are experts in this field of work, with a team of dedicated professionals who are experienced in integrating people with disabilities into the workplace.

description_full:
Where we meet
We can meet you at a location that is convenient for you. Whether it's a cafe, library or college, we'll make sure it's a place you feel comfortable to talk.
Need support?

https://www.choicesupport.org.uk/find-support/find-support-near-you/richmond-wandsworth
How we can help
We will work with you to write an individual support plan with goals and timescales.
We will look at your work goals and provide support to develop your skills. This could mean doing work trials, work experience, education, training or voluntary work.
We run job clubs so that you can meet others who may be looking for work.
We will signpost you to organisations or resources to support particular needs.
We will provide flexible support depending on where you are on your employment journey.

Avenues works with young people and adults with autism who have complex needs or behaviour that challenges. We can support people at home, to get involved in life outside the home, or, where appropriate for some adults, in residential settings.
Because everyone has different support needs and their reasons for seeking support vary, we start by building relationships with people based on trust and understanding, including with family members. name:
Sidcup- Choice Support
Carlton Centre
description_full:
Based in Sidcup, we provide a wide range of opportunities for adults with autism and learning disabilities both at the centre itself and in the community.
Join Carlton
Get in touch
We help people to learn new skills and increase their confidence, through activity sessions at the centre or at community venues. We have minibuses to get to some of the venues or we use public transport.
Can we support you?
Yes, as long as you are over 18 years old and have autism or learning disabilities. You will need to pay for the activities and you can do that if you have your own funding or if you have been assessed for funding through your local council.
https://www.choicesupport.org.uk/find-support/find-support-near-you/carlton
Our activities
We decide the activities based on your feedback and your needs, and so that you can try new experiences.
We have a full programme of activities from cooking and computer skills to drama and drum beats. You will have a timetable of activities to choose from.
There is a small extra charge for some of our activities and we will tell you this when you join us.

description_full:
Individual placement and support - helping you find the right job
Do you live in Wandsworth?
Do you want to find a job?
If your answer to all of these questions is YES, Share's Employment Specialist can help you.
What we offer:
-Benefits advice
-A CV review and updating service
-Help with writing a covering letter and filling in application forms
-Help with your job search and advice on where to look for jobs
-Advice on how to prepare for interviews, including conducting mock interviews
-Links with potential employers in Wandsworth
We focus on the jobs you want and tailor job search sessions to meet your needs. You will receive support throughout the recruitment process to ensure you find the right job for you.
How to sign up
To take advantage of Share's FREE employment support service, you must be a Wandsworth resident with a mental health concern that is being accessed and managed by a Wandsworth community mental health team.
Ask your care coordinator or key worker to refer you to Share’s Employment Specialist, Hannah Christian, email:

tel: 0207 801 9823 or mobile 07445 365 365.

Westminster Kingsway College offers provision for 16-24 year old students with dedicated courses for students with learning disabilities and/or difficulties. These courses are designed to meet the requirements of the new Foundation Learning Framework and include an emphasis on personal and social development, life and money skills and employability skills where relevant to the student.
Students who apply for a course will undertake an assessment to make sure that they are on the right course to meet their learning needs with support provided to make sure that we help them succeed on their chosen course. Students who successfully complete the course could progress to a higher level or to voluntary or supported work.

name: Asperger London Area Group -ALAG
description_full:
ALAG runs a monthly support group for adults on the autistic spectrum living in the Greater London area although those from elsewhere are welcome. It is a chance to meet others to talk about common issues and to support and help each other. The first half-hour of the meeting is spent socialising and having refreshments. This is followed by the whole group sitting down together to discuss an agreed topic or to hear from different speakers who offer advice and guidance on issues relating to autism. These topics are planned in advance and any member can suggest a future topic.
The group meets on the first Tuesday of every month in Islington from 6.30pm to 8.30pm. Booking in advance for new attendees is essential. There is a small entrance fee of £3 (members) or £4 (non-members) to cover the cost of refreshments, including a vegetarian option.
Email: info@alag.org.uk
Telephone: 07435 560 568
Website: www.alag.org.uk
Registered Charity address:
ALAG
c/o Disability Action in Islington
90–92 Upper Street
London N1 0NP
(visitors by appointment ONLY)

description_full:
An truly inclusive autism association
AIM Autistic Community Group
We provide social opportunities for both adults and children along with support for parents and carers aiding families and individuals to access the community in accepting inclusive circumstances.
We currently run two groups one is every Tuesday 4-6pm at Brook Hill Children’s Centre, Brook Hill Road, Woolwich, London SE18 6UZ,
& every Friday 10-12am at Trinity Centre, 165 Burrage Road, Plumstead, London SE18 7JW.
Refreshments inclusive of price, tea, coffee, biscuits, fruit and squash.
There is no age limit on our two groups and siblings are welcome .
There is sensory play provided each week to aid and stimulate the tactile, auditory, visual and olfactory senses including a home made play dough that is safe for oral seekers.
Referral not necessary pre diagnosis welcome.
Brook Hill group is £2 per family and Trinity is £3.
For more info email autisticinclusivemeets@gmail.com

gmaps_reviews_top3: TOM ALO WM(1): My experience with Resources for Autism has unfortunately been extremely disappointing and frustrating. From the outset, the service has felt chaotic and poorly managed.  Firstly, the communication system is unnecessarily confusing. Having two separate phone numbers for London and Birmingham makes little sense. A single phone line with clear menu options would be far more effective. When I called, the staff member I spoke to seemed uninterested, unwilling to answer questions, and simply tried to pass me on. Staff should be trained and equipped with knowledge about both branches so that they can provide consistent, accurate information. Instead, I was left feeling dismissed and unsupported.  When I raised my concerns, I was told to email my details so someone could call me back. I waited over a month with no response. Only after I chased it up did anyone acknowledge my email. Even then, I was told that I shouldn’t have been promised a call in the first place—something I found very unprofessional. If a service promises to follow up, it should follow through. Ignoring emails—especially when they outline personal struggles and support needs—is unacceptable. A simple acknowledgment message or short response would have been far better than silence.  The overall handling of my case has been poor: •	Promises were made that were not kept. •	My email was ignored until I chased it. •	Staff appeared untrained, disinterested, and inconsistent in their information. •	Communication across the organisation seemed disjointed, from reception to admin to management.  I had real hopes that Resources for Autism could provide meaningful support. Instead, I felt let down, fobbed off, and left questioning whether an organisation that cannot manage basic communication is truly autism-friendly.  What should have happened is simple: someone should have explained what services are available, clarified the referral process, and set realistic expectations. That would have gone a long way to building trust. Instead, the lack of professionalism and broken promises have completely undermined my confidence in the service.  I strongly urge Resources for Autism to reflect on: •	Communication processes (including phone lines and email responses). •	Staff training, ensuring all staff can provide clear and consistent information. •	Transparency about services offered, without overpromising or backtracking. •	Building an approach that is genuinely autism-friendly, which means clarity, consistency, and reliability.  At present, my experience has been so negative that I would not recommend Resources for Autism. Unless these serious issues are addressed, others will likely feel the same disappointment and frustration that I did. | Michelle(1): I reached out in february, because I felt the signs of an approaching autistic burnout. I was hoping to learn any copying mechanisms, or at least receive some sort of support / someone to talk to without judgement, which is according to their website something they take pride in.  I had a phone call with someone who said they'll put me on the waiting list for therapy, and they'll send me some helpful information.  The information provided was unfortunately hardly of any use, and I don't think they put me on the waiting list at all. They told me they'd get back to me in October for therapy. It is December as I am writing this and I still haven't heard back from them, even after reaching out per email last month...  In the end, I did burn out, lost my job, and now struggle to find a new one, which is exactly what I wanted to avoid by reaching out, so thanks for nothing! | Sa ku Ra(5): Their staff are all well trained and friendly.  My son had a great experience with them.

gmaps_reviews_top3: TOM ALO WM(1): My experience with Resources for Autism has unfortunately been extremely disappointing and frustrating. From the outset, the service has felt chaotic and poorly managed.  Firstly, the communication system is unnecessarily confusing. Having two separate phone numbers for London and Birmingham makes little sense. A single phone line with clear menu options would be far more effective. When I called, the staff member I spoke to seemed uninterested, unwilling to answer questions, and simply tried to pass me on. Staff should be trained and equipped with knowledge about both branches so that they can provide consistent, accurate information. Instead, I was left feeling dismissed and unsupported.  When I raised my concerns, I was told to email my details so someone could call me back. I waited over a month with no response. Only after I chased it up did anyone acknowledge my email. Even then, I was told that I shouldn’t have been promised a call in the first place—something I found very unprofessional. If a service promises to follow up, it should follow through. Ignoring emails—especially when they outline personal struggles and support needs—is unacceptable. A simple acknowledgment message or short response would have been far better than silence.  The overall handling of my case has been poor: •	Promises were made that were not kept. •	My email was ignored until I chased it. •	Staff appeared untrained, disinterested, and inconsistent in their information. •	Communication across the organisation seemed disjointed, from reception to admin to management.  I had real hopes that Resources for Autism could provide meaningful support. Instead, I felt let down, fobbed off, and left questioning whether an organisation that cannot manage basic communication is truly autism-friendly.  What should have happened is simple: someone should have explained what services are available, clarified the referral process, and set realistic expectations. That would have gone a long way to building trust. Instead, the lack of professionalism and broken promises have completely undermined my confidence in the service.  I strongly urge Resources for Autism to reflect on: •	Communication processes (including phone lines and email responses). •	Staff training, ensuring all staff can provide clear and consistent information. •	Transparency about services offered, without overpromising or backtracking. •	Building an approach that is genuinely autism-friendly, which means clarity, consistency, and reliability.  At present, my experience has been so negative that I would not recommend Resources for Autism. Unless these serious issues are addressed, others will likely feel the same disappointment and frustration that I did. | Michelle(1): I reached out in february, because I felt the signs of an approaching autistic burnout. I was hoping to learn any copying mechanisms, or at least receive some sort of support / someone to talk to without judgement, which is according to their website something they take pride in.  I had a phone call with someone who said they'll put me on the waiting list for therapy, and they'll send me some helpful information.  The information provided was unfortunately hardly of any use, and I don't think they put me on the waiting list at all. They told me they'd get back to me in October for therapy. It is December as I am writing this and I still haven't heard back from them, even after reaching out per email last month...  In the end, I did burn out, lost my job, and now struggle to find a new one, which is exactly what I wanted to avoid by reaching out, so thanks for nothing! | Sa ku Ra(5): Their staff are all well trained and friendly.  My son had a great experience with them.

gmaps_reviews_top3: TOM ALO WM(1): My experience with Resources for Autism has unfortunately been extremely disappointing and frustrating. From the outset, the service has felt chaotic and poorly managed.  Firstly, the communication system is unnecessarily confusing. Having two separate phone numbers for London and Birmingham makes little sense. A single phone line with clear menu options would be far more effective. When I called, the staff member I spoke to seemed uninterested, unwilling to answer questions, and simply tried to pass me on. Staff should be trained and equipped with knowledge about both branches so that they can provide consistent, accurate information. Instead, I was left feeling dismissed and unsupported.  When I raised my concerns, I was told to email my details so someone could call me back. I waited over a month with no response. Only after I chased it up did anyone acknowledge my email. Even then, I was told that I shouldn’t have been promised a call in the first place—something I found very unprofessional. If a service promises to follow up, it should follow through. Ignoring emails—especially when they outline personal struggles and support needs—is unacceptable. A simple acknowledgment message or short response would have been far better than silence.  The overall handling of my case has been poor: •	Promises were made that were not kept. •	My email was ignored until I chased it. •	Staff appeared untrained, disinterested, and inconsistent in their information. •	Communication across the organisation seemed disjointed, from reception to admin to management.  I had real hopes that Resources for Autism could provide meaningful support. Instead, I felt let down, fobbed off, and left questioning whether an organisation that cannot manage basic communication is truly autism-friendly.  What should have happened is simple: someone should have explained what services are available, clarified the referral process, and set realistic expectations. That would have gone a long way to building trust. Instead, the lack of professionalism and broken promises have completely undermined my confidence in the service.  I strongly urge Resources for Autism to reflect on: •	Communication processes (including phone lines and email responses). •	Staff training, ensuring all staff can provide clear and consistent information. •	Transparency about services offered, without overpromising or backtracking. •	Building an approach that is genuinely autism-friendly, which means clarity, consistency, and reliability.  At present, my experience has been so negative that I would not recommend Resources for Autism. Unless these serious issues are addressed, others will likely feel the same disappointment and frustration that I did. | Michelle(1): I reached out in february, because I felt the signs of an approaching autistic burnout. I was hoping to learn any copying mechanisms, or at least receive some sort of support / someone to talk to without judgement, which is according to their website something they take pride in.  I had a phone call with someone who said they'll put me on the waiting list for therapy, and they'll send me some helpful information.  The information provided was unfortunately hardly of any use, and I don't think they put me on the waiting list at all. They told me they'd get back to me in October for therapy. It is December as I am writing this and I still haven't heard back from them, even after reaching out per email last month...  In the end, I did burn out, lost my job, and now struggle to find a new one, which is exactly what I wanted to avoid by reaching out, so thanks for nothing! | Sa ku Ra(5): Their staff are all well trained and friendly.  My son had a great experience with them.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Sakina Bhaloo(5): Jacqui was at the very start of our journey with my son who is on the spectrum . She spent over an hour at our initial consult explaining what my son might be experiencing and steps we needed to take to help him, and a lot of positivity when we needed it most ! In two months of OT and in combination with a kinesiologist she recommended my son has made tangible improvements. Our OT Georgia picked up key areas to start working on and my son enjoys his sessions with her and her magic swing greatly ! Thank you Jacqui and the whole team at Kids in Sync! | Kim Hamilton-Winch(5): I am so delighted with my son’s transformation over the course of his OT therapy the past couple of years at Kids in Sync. I had really hoped to see some changes in his speech and use of language, which i definitely have noticed, but the unexpected changes have been to his thinking, understanding and learning abilities. He is just processing everything much better and much faster and, perhaps linked to this, there has been a real step-change in his mood and general confidence too. It feels like more options have opened up for him now, and I am really excited about his next chapter. Jacqui is fantastic and has a wealth of knowledge and experience to explore every possibility to help in supporting and developing further, do not hesitate if your looking for help please contact Kids in Sync as they truly care and support your child as an individual and want the best as we do as parents.

gmaps_reviews_top3: TOM ALO WM(1): My experience with Resources for Autism has unfortunately been extremely disappointing and frustrating. From the outset, the service has felt chaotic and poorly managed.  Firstly, the communication system is unnecessarily confusing. Having two separate phone numbers for London and Birmingham makes little sense. A single phone line with clear menu options would be far more effective. When I called, the staff member I spoke to seemed uninterested, unwilling to answer questions, and simply tried to pass me on. Staff should be trained and equipped with knowledge about both branches so that they can provide consistent, accurate information. Instead, I was left feeling dismissed and unsupported.  When I raised my concerns, I was told to email my details so someone could call me back. I waited over a month with no response. Only after I chased it up did anyone acknowledge my email. Even then, I was told that I shouldn’t have been promised a call in the first place—something I found very unprofessional. If a service promises to follow up, it should follow through. Ignoring emails—especially when they outline personal struggles and support needs—is unacceptable. A simple acknowledgment message or short response would have been far better than silence.  The overall handling of my case has been poor: •	Promises were made that were not kept. •	My email was ignored until I chased it. •	Staff appeared untrained, disinterested, and inconsistent in their information. •	Communication across the organisation seemed disjointed, from reception to admin to management.  I had real hopes that Resources for Autism could provide meaningful support. Instead, I felt let down, fobbed off, and left questioning whether an organisation that cannot manage basic communication is truly autism-friendly.  What should have happened is simple: someone should have explained what services are available, clarified the referral process, and set realistic expectations. That would have gone a long way to building trust. Instead, the lack of professionalism and broken promises have completely undermined my confidence in the service.  I strongly urge Resources for Autism to reflect on: •	Communication processes (including phone lines and email responses). •	Staff training, ensuring all staff can provide clear and consistent information. •	Transparency about services offered, without overpromising or backtracking. •	Building an approach that is genuinely autism-friendly, which means clarity, consistency, and reliability.  At present, my experience has been so negative that I would not recommend Resources for Autism. Unless these serious issues are addressed, others will likely feel the same disappointment and frustration that I did. | Michelle(1): I reached out in february, because I felt the signs of an approaching autistic burnout. I was hoping to learn any copying mechanisms, or at least receive some sort of support / someone to talk to without judgement, which is according to their website something they take pride in.  I had a phone call with someone who said they'll put me on the waiting list for therapy, and they'll send me some helpful information.  The information provided was unfortunately hardly of any use, and I don't think they put me on the waiting list at all. They told me they'd get back to me in October for therapy. It is December as I am writing this and I still haven't heard back from them, even after reaching out per email last month...  In the end, I did burn out, lost my job, and now struggle to find a new one, which is exactly what I wanted to avoid by reaching out, so thanks for nothing! | Sa ku Ra(5): Their staff are all well trained and friendly.  My son had a great experience with them.

name: Bexley Voice Parent Carer Forum
description_full:
We are a group of voluntary parents/carers of children with disabilities, special or additional needs who live in Bexley, Kent. The ages of the children and young people we represent are between 0-25.
We proudly became a registered charity in 2011.
Postal Address:
BEXLEY VOICE
C/O SEND HUB -
NORMANDY CHILDRENS CENTRE
FAIRFORD AVENUE, BARNEHURST,
KENT. DA7 6QP
Email: bexleyvoice@hotmail.co.uk
Telephone Helpline: 07512 409 936
(Please note this is answered by volunteers.
Please leave a message if unanswered and we will return your call as soon as possible.)
OUR AIMS
To represent families by providing a "voice" to express their views and opinions, to help improve the lives of children with special needs.
We do this by:
1. Attending meetings with Bexley Council for the development and provision of services for our children and young people.
2. Providing a central parent consultation point for Bexley Council and service providers.
3. Proactively providing ongoing feedback to service providers on performance.
4. Raising awareness of the impact of a wide range of special needs on family life.
5. Providing views, based on personal experiences and canvassed opinion, ensuring that all Parents and Carers, whatever their background, have the opportunity to have their opinions represented.
WHAT DO WE DO?
Bexley Voice:
-Attend regular meetings with service providers, such as Bexley Council, providing feedback to them about the needs that our children have.
-Provide a central consultation point for any organisation that needs advice on children or young people with special needs and/or disabilities.
-Signpost parents and carers who need contact information for local and national organisations, groups and charities.
-Have visited over 40 schools in Bexley as part of our School Visit Programme.
-Organised 3 Parent/Carer Conferences and over 30 parent and carer workshops on many different subjects such as the SEN Reforms.
-Support families by telephone, email and via our large information website and secret Facebook group.
WHEN DO WE MEET?
Bexley Voice would like to invite you to our "Tea and Talk Parent/Carer Support" group. It is a good time to come along, meet our members, have an informal chat over a cup of tea and discuss any issues you may have. We can offer advice on various issues such as statements/EHC Plans, transport, transition, schools, benefits and activities.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Aishah Al Nahyan(1): I have used the Asian Disabled Persons Association (APDA) care services for some time and am currently still under contract with them. I want to acknowledge that the standard of personal care I’ve received has generally been good — that’s not where the issue lies.  However, there are several recurring concerns around management, complaints handling, communication, and flexibility, particularly for service users on Direct Payments.  1. Lack of Management Accessibility When there is a problem or complaint, it’s virtually impossible to get past the single point of contact assigned to you. Managers do not return calls, do not engage directly, and do not provide independent review or oversight. Everything is filtered through one person, which creates a closed system with no accountability or escalation route.  2. Misunderstanding of Direct Payment Arrangements APDA often behaves as if the contract is still between them and social services, rather than between them and me as the Direct Payment holder. This causes confusion and breaches privacy boundaries, as they act as if they are accountable to social services when, in fact, my contract is directly with them.  3. Lack of Flexibility and Backup Care There’s no operational flexibility when I need to adjust hours or arrange occasional extra support. They have limited or no backup care staff available, which leaves me without care and support when something unexpected happens.  This raises safeguarding concerns, as I have been left without care or support despite my needs being known and assessed. There’s no visible awareness of the risks this creates for a disabled client.  4. Profit Over People Mindset Despite being described as a non-profit organisation, their behaviour often reflects a profit-driven mindset — focusing on payments and financial control rather than care continuity or client wellbeing. It makes me question whether the organisation’s stated values align with their actual practice.  In summary, while APDA can provide good care day-to-day, the organisation struggles when there’s a challenge, complaint, or need for flexibility. A good provider should be able to handle issues constructively, but in my experience, APDA resists change, transparency, and open dialogue.  When something goes wrong ego takes presidence. | Rajendrakumar Patel(5): | Asif Salim(4):

gmaps_reviews_top3: TOM ALO WM(1): My experience with Resources for Autism has unfortunately been extremely disappointing and frustrating. From the outset, the service has felt chaotic and poorly managed.  Firstly, the communication system is unnecessarily confusing. Having two separate phone numbers for London and Birmingham makes little sense. A single phone line with clear menu options would be far more effective. When I called, the staff member I spoke to seemed uninterested, unwilling to answer questions, and simply tried to pass me on. Staff should be trained and equipped with knowledge about both branches so that they can provide consistent, accurate information. Instead, I was left feeling dismissed and unsupported.  When I raised my concerns, I was told to email my details so someone could call me back. I waited over a month with no response. Only after I chased it up did anyone acknowledge my email. Even then, I was told that I shouldn’t have been promised a call in the first place—something I found very unprofessional. If a service promises to follow up, it should follow through. Ignoring emails—especially when they outline personal struggles and support needs—is unacceptable. A simple acknowledgment message or short response would have been far better than silence.  The overall handling of my case has been poor: •	Promises were made that were not kept. •	My email was ignored until I chased it. •	Staff appeared untrained, disinterested, and inconsistent in their information. •	Communication across the organisation seemed disjointed, from reception to admin to management.  I had real hopes that Resources for Autism could provide meaningful support. Instead, I felt let down, fobbed off, and left questioning whether an organisation that cannot manage basic communication is truly autism-friendly.  What should have happened is simple: someone should have explained what services are available, clarified the referral process, and set realistic expectations. That would have gone a long way to building trust. Instead, the lack of professionalism and broken promises have completely undermined my confidence in the service.  I strongly urge Resources for Autism to reflect on: •	Communication processes (including phone lines and email responses). •	Staff training, ensuring all staff can provide clear and consistent information. •	Transparency about services offered, without overpromising or backtracking. •	Building an approach that is genuinely autism-friendly, which means clarity, consistency, and reliability.  At present, my experience has been so negative that I would not recommend Resources for Autism. Unless these serious issues are addressed, others will likely feel the same disappointment and frustration that I did. | Michelle(1): I reached out in february, because I felt the signs of an approaching autistic burnout. I was hoping to learn any copying mechanisms, or at least receive some sort of support / someone to talk to without judgement, which is according to their website something they take pride in.  I had a phone call with someone who said they'll put me on the waiting list for therapy, and they'll send me some helpful information.  The information provided was unfortunately hardly of any use, and I don't think they put me on the waiting list at all. They told me they'd get back to me in October for therapy. It is December as I am writing this and I still haven't heard back from them, even after reaching out per email last month...  In the end, I did burn out, lost my job, and now struggle to find a new one, which is exactly what I wanted to avoid by reaching out, so thanks for nothing! | Sa ku Ra(5): Their staff are all well trained and friendly.  My son had a great experience with them.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Jade Rispoli(5): Without CASPA I don't know where we would be! The staff are amazing and make you feel like part of the family from the minute you meet them, the help and support they have given my family has been nothing short of amazing. Then there are the other families you meet at CASPA,  and the way we all help and support eachother from the minute we first met. I know I've made some amazing friends here and my son has too. I have never been particularly comfortable in social situations but I've never once felt out of place or awkward at CASPA. I have some disaster beach stories and was very uneasy at the thought of a CASPA beach day with members of all age groups all meeting together at the beach, within 2 minutes of being there I was completely at ease, the staff and other families who I hadn't even yet met was so helpful and friendly and just knowing thar everyone there "gets it" made me feel so much more relaxed and I had the best beach day I've ever had with my little family and my 2 older children got more of my time than usual at the beach because there was staff everywhere to offer help when needed and I just relaxed and had a great time wich is very rare. I cannot recommend CASPA enough and I I cannot thank the CASPA team enough for all that they do, they are truly the most amazing people. | Henry Marsh(5): CASPA has been an absolute blessing for my niece and entire family. The warmth, understanding, and professionalism displayed by the staff have made a significant positive impact on her life. They provide a safe and nurturing environment where she can express herself freely, make friends, and develop important life skills. The diverse range of activities and programs offered are thoughtfully designed to cater to the unique needs of individuals. Whether it's the engaging social clubs, the creative arts sessions, or the advocacy workshops, each aspect of CASPA's services has helped my niece grow in confidence and independence. It's not just a support network; it's a community where she feels accepted and valued. As a family, we are incredibly grateful for the sense of pride and empowerment CASPA has instilled in our niece. The staff’s genuine care and dedication have provided us with peace of mind, knowing she's in a place where she is understood. CASPA truly embodies what it means to support and advocate for individuals with autism. name: Camden special parents forum
description_full:
Camden special parents forum
As part of our parent and family support services, Camden Special Parents’ Forum is a growing network of over 250 parents and carers of 0-25 year olds with special education needs and disabilities.
The forum runs meetings and training workshops for parents to help them to access the information and skills they need to support their families. Anyone who is looking after a child or young person with special needs (who lives or goes to school in Camden) is welcome to join the Forum, either as a regular or occasional visitor. We will make you welcome and you can be involved in our activities as much as you want.
The Forum is facilitated by parents with children with special and complex needs.
