name: Chalfont St Peter Youth Centre
description_full:
Holds a weekly session for young people with ASD from 7-8pm on Tuesdays during term time.
The sessions are supervised by trained adults but benefit enormously from a team of trained young volunteer helpers. These helpers are on hand for each session to interact with the young people attending, encouraging play and social skills by modelling behaviour.
The youth club has a state of the art, fenced outdoor AstroTurf sports court enabling the young people to play football, basketball etc. Also air hockey, pool, table football, arts, craft and Lego are available at each session.
The youth centre is the holder of the Queen's Award, the MBE for voluntary services which it received in 2013.
Please call the office on 01753 883712 or email office@cspyouthcentre.org.uk for further information.
Tithe Barn
Sandy Rise
Chalfont St Peter
Bucks SL9 9TR
full_address: The club is aimed at young people from Year 5-Year 8 approximately and has been successfully running for around 2 years. It aims to provide a safe and calmer environment for young people who wish to socialise but perhaps struggle to cope with larger, louder environments.
content:
Holds a weekly session for young people with ASD from 7-8pm on Tuesdays during term time.
The club is aimed at young people from Year 5-Year 8 approximately and has been successfully running for around 2 years. It aims to provide a safe and calmer environment for young people who wish to socialise but perhaps struggle to cope with larger, louder environments.
The sessions are supervised by trained adults but benefit enormously from a team of trained young volunteer helpers. These helpers are on hand for each session to interact with the young people attending, encouraging play and social skills by modelling behaviour.
The youth club has a state of the art, fenced outdoor AstroTurf sports court enabling the young people to play football, basketball etc. Also air hockey, pool, table football, arts, craft and Lego are available at each session.
The youth centre is the holder of the Queen's Award, the MBE for voluntary services which it received in 2013.
 
Please call the office on 01753 883712 or email office@cspyouthcentre.org.uk for further information.

Tithe Barn
Sandy Rise
Chalfont St Peter
Bucks SL9 9TR

gmaps_reviews_top3: Skram Jax(5): In all my years going to London, NEVER entertained The Barbican Centre....silly me! Absolutely amazed at this great space, building, history, eateries, events & activities & landscape where concrete, water & greenery create surprising pleasure to the eye.  Great cosmopolitan chilled out vibes. Watching your money, take a picnic! Free to enter The Barbican itself to have a mooch around 😍 | Edith Finch(4): Very big area and interesting experiment. It’s nice for the residents here to have a quiet zone in the center of a big city. But honestly the architecture doesn’t feel welcoming and walking down those big long corridors is a bit scary because there is not a single soul around as the shops area and the residence area are strictly separated (resembles many places in the US). On the other hand, the shopping area is nice. They have galleries, theaters, gift store with many unique products and a cafe (food is ok but the portion is abundant). | Yaya Thanks(5): I recently visited for a work conference and completely forgot how special of a space the centre is, particularly in the city of London where it sticks out like a sore thumb.  The main event hall for one is spectacular with great views and acoustics, not to mention comfy seats (for the first hour).  The staff are very friendly if not high brow.  I was also surprisingly impressed by their toilets, but the best part is undoubtedly the outdoor space of the garden and pond area, which is open to the public (not the garden).  Highly recommend for a city lunch/chat if the weather is nice.
gmaps_editorial_summary: Cultural venue with concert hall, theatres, cinema, galleries and home of London Symphony Orchestra.

name: City of London Parent Carer Forum
description_full:
City Parents & Carers
We are working with the City of London and the City and Hackney CCG to ensure that our voices are heard when decisions are being made which may affect our children and young people. We are represented on the City of London’s SEND Programme Board.
Parents and carers of children and young people who have an Education Health and Care plan have been meeting once a term with local authority officers to talk about the services we would like to see for our children and young people. We are developing and growing to include parents and carers of all children and young people with SEND regardless of whether they have an EHC plan.
As we grow we will be:
• Organising activities for our children and families
• Organising events and training days
• Linking with forums in other parts of the UK to share ideas

name: Croydon- Jubilee Chill’n’Chat Group
description_full:
Chill’n’Chat Special is weekly group for parents and carers of children with special needs. We
recognise that all children are unique and they all develop and learn at different rates. Your
The Programme – To make this group as relevant as possible for those attending, the
programme is decided by the parents. We like to have a mixture of speakers from other
organisations, (National Autistic Society, Parents in Partnership, Paediatric Physiotherapists
and Parent Partnerships), parenting topics (building self-esteem, managing behaviour,
parenting styles etc.) and treats (crafts, card making, manicures, hand massages etc.).
Current Venues:-
Woodlands Children’s Centre, Selsdon CR2 8HD Tuesday morning 9.30-11.30
Kensington Avenue Children’s Centre, CR7 8BT – Wednesday afternoons 1.00-2.45
These groups meet term time only. Creche places may be available if booked in advance.
Jubilee Parenting - have been supporting parents in Croydon for about 12 years. We run a
range of parenting courses alongside our weekly Chill’n’Chats. We aim to provide information,
practical support and some relaxation time for parents who are often under pressure.
PRISM – is a consortium of organisations working together to support parents of children
with special needs.
If you would like more information or to be added to our email circulation, please contact
Jane Carey or Rachel Cummings
email: Jane.carey@croydon-jubilee.co.uk / Rachel.cummings@croydon-jubilee.co.uk

Our Services:
Chill and Chat
Free support, advice and a friendly face in a safe and enabling environment for families who have a child with a learning disability.
Leslie Park
The Leslie Park Project provides day activities for younger aged adults (18-40 years of age).
Older Carers
The Carer’s Assessment and Support Service (CASS) offers help and advice to carers aged over 50 who look after someone with learning disability.
Carers Welfare
The Carer’s Welfare Advice Service offers information, advice and guidance to carers who are under 50 years of age, who look after someone with a learning disability.
Social Club
The Monday Night Social Club – social contact, activities and fun for adults aged 18+.
‘Just for us’ Discos
We hold six discos a year for adults who have a learning disability aged 18+.
Learning Disability Forum
A meeting for people who have a learning disability and their carers – often attended by health professionals, council members and speakers.
Welfare Benefits
The Welfare Benefits Advice and Information Service provides benefits advice for people with learning disabilities and their families.
The Saturday Club
A club for young adults who have a learning disability, aged 18+.

The Monday Night Social Club – social contact, activities and fun for adults aged 18+.
 
‘Just for us’ Discos
We hold six discos a year for adults who have a learning disability aged 18+.
 
Learning Disability Forum
A meeting for people who have a learning disability and their carers – often attended by health professionals, council members and speakers.
 
Welfare Benefits
The Welfare Benefits Advice and Information Service provides benefits advice for people with learning disabilities and their families.
 
The Saturday Club
A club for young adults who have a learning disability, aged 18+.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Matanda Besa(1): If I could give this practice zero stars, I absolutely would.  From start to finish, my experience was nothing short of abysmal. If the CQC were to conduct an inspection, I have no doubt they would rate this practice as poor at best.  The receptionist I encountered was shockingly unprofessional. Despite providing all the necessary information to locate my appointment, she proceeded to loudly announce the reason for my visit—completely disregarding patient confidentiality. This level of carelessness is unacceptable in any healthcare setting.  Unfortunately, the unprofessionalism did not end there. The nurse (or at least, I assume they were a nurse, as they were not wearing a name tag and failed to introduce themselves) demonstrated the same lack of professionalism throughout my consultation.  As someone who works in a hospital, I am well aware of the standards and expectations in a healthcare setting. I would not have written this review if previous experiences at this practice had been better. However, every encounter I have had with this practice in the last 6 months has been a demonstration of similar  poor behaviour/attitudes. I am not surprised to see similar reviews from other patients...  These issues are clearly not isolated incidents, raising serious concerns about the overall standard of care at this practice, a follow up will be sent to the GM. | Scott Pennell(1): Technology booking system is not doing its job. Text Appointment bookings are not working with the surgery. You end waiting for a phone call that the surgery don’t even know about. Then you need to revert to the old fashioned method of walking in to speak to a human anyways. If you upgrade to a fully integrated technology booking system at least make sure it works first!! Wasting time is frustrating. | Anthony Barr(1): Absolutely has to be the worst Surgery there is. Collectively the practice seems to not really understand patient care and while not all processes can be put as blame to the practice it is so stodgy - unnecessary delays - unhelpful staff, shocking, really is shocking.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Victoria L(5): Prompt, professional and very friendly, warm and welcoming. A great service from these professionals. Both the Dr and Charlie the Speech & Language therapist were brilliant with my 4 year old who had a great time with them. | hafiza bashir(5): Charlie and Dr Clements were amazing. So friendly and professional. Charlie was brilliant with our son and he actually did not want to leave as he had so much fun. He has an amazing way with children.  The parent questionnaire was done over the phone beforehand by Dr Clements which I thought was brilliant as my child didn’t have to sit through this and hear us discuss his traits and behaviour.  I was hesitant about seeking a private autism diagnosis as we had been told that these were not accepted by other professionals but we decided to go ahead as we felt it would be helpful for us to know if our son had autism. We have since had our initial assessment with the community paediatrician who has reviewed the report from LEAD, agreeing with the diagnosis and the recommendations within it as it is so comprehensive. This means we do not have to wait another two years for a diagnosis.  I absolutely recommend the team at LEAD. | Wends S(5): Would like the thank LEAD for seeing my brother back in February 2024. He was 18 at the time, just about made it as the cut off point to use this service is 18. Had to go through the private route due to CAHMS not accepting our first referral which took about 6 months to get that answer! The telephone and face to face assessment all happened pretty much within a month. My brother got his ASD diagnosis straight after the face to face assessment and was sent a report the next day. Very professional, worth every penny. Thank you again!

name: The Larches Trust -Edgware
description_full:
The Larches Community is a registered charity established in 1995 by a group of families to create opportunities for young people and adults with learning disabilities and/or Autism and Asperger's syndrome.
www.larchescommunity.org.uk
Tel: +44 20 8905 6333
Objectives
1.Education and Training: To increase the skills base of young people and adults with learning disabilities through education and training so that they can enjoy greater independence in their lives.
2.Social and Recreational: To increase the quality of life of young people and adults with learning disabilities through social and recreational activities.
3.Social Enterprise: To become increasingly financially independent as an organisation by developing social enterprises and micro businesses.
4.Supported Living: To give people with learning disabilities the opportunity to live independent lives in the heart of the community by establishing supported living homes.
5.Community Inclusion: To combat prejudice within the mainstream community by increasing understanding of learning disabilities and autism.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Mr. Puffin(1): I started going to ENAS groups after coming to terms with being autistic later in life, although I really didn’t have a good time here and ended up leaving in the end. The whole thing just felt to clinical and patronising to me and I found myself feeling judged and disrespected for being autistic anyway which made me uncomfortable.  I also didn’t seem to get on with the woman who runs these groups either even though she apparently has a child with autism, which I felt may have been a big part of her motivation for being involved with NAS in the first place. In terms of trying to understand her own child and work out how high functioning they were in comparison to other autistic people, or even to try and fix autistic people in some sense and make them more ‘normal’ in quite an ambitious and egotistical way. Which is completely the wrong attitude to have about autism anyway as far as I am concerned, since you can’t be something that you are not or pretend to be a horse when you are really a zebra.  I don’t know if this is reflective of NAS in general or if it is just a problem with the Enfield branch, but I could tell that many of the ideas and approaches that they had to autism here were not really accurate at all or even verging on delusional. So if this is the general outlook that NAS has about autism, then I just find it amazing that they have absolutely no understanding or insight into this sort of thing and are this ignorant. As I found it to be undermining and insulting rather than helpful, even after I tried to bring up these issues and improve communication.  To make matters even worse though was the fact that one of the other people who was working for ENAS and seemed to be very much involved with it was someone who used to actually make fun of me for being autistic or projected his own issues about it onto me and was very nice at all, and also doesn’t seem to have changed that much during this time. But apparently ENAS doesn’t seem to notice these things and be aware of such attitudes, and so could even be potentially enabling abusive situations to happen. Since I even ended up feeling worse in some ways through having joined when I was really hoping for something that would be more helpful.

description_full:
Enfield Mencap
General Telephone No: 020 8887 9980
E-mail: enquiries@enfieldmencap.org.uk
http://www.enfieldmencap.org.uk/
Saturday Respite
This is a service providing respite to parents and carers supporting learning-disabled adults living at home in the London Borough of Enfield. The service is currently free at the point of delivery, being funded through the Enfield Carers’ Grant.
The service is provided every Saturday (excluding Easter, Christmas and New Year) 10:00 am–5:00 pm. Unlike other Enfield Mencap services, no transport to and from the service is provided.
Club 18+
A dedicated after-school/college club for young adults in transition from Children’s to Adult services. The Club currently operates from The New Opportunity Centre . It begins at 3:30pm and continues through to 7:00pm each evening.
Gateway Clubs
Enfield Mencap is pleased to support the three Gateway Clubs operating in the Borough.
The three Clubs operate in different parts of the Borough on Tuesday, Wednesday and Thursday evenings. They provide recreational facilities for people with learning disabilities.

name: Enfield-Our Voice
description_full:
We are a parent–led organisation, working with parents and carers, as well as the statutory and voluntary sectors.
Tel: 07516 662315
Email: info @ ourvoiceenfield.org.uk
Aims
Our Voice aims to improve services for children and young people with disabilities or special educational needs in the borough of Enfield.
To do this we:
We negotiate representation on decision-making bodies
We invite service providers to consult us on local and national issues affecting our children and seek a can-do approach from them
We ask children, parents and carers to tell us about common concerns
We inform & support families though our newsletters, website, workshops and meetings
We campaign to change attitudes and remove barriers
We monitor changes in services, lobby for improvements and recognise and promote good practices
We help empower all parents of children with special needs and disabilities
We offer the following services:
-Family Network support help line
-Disability Network Meetings
-Information Newsletters
-Consultancy Services; e.g. running Parent consultations, or providing Training for Parents and Professionals

name: Essex carers’ Support
description_full:
Essex carers’ Support are funded by and working with Essex County Council to provide Advice information and guidance to Carers to:
-Improve the quality of life for carers.
-Reduce Isolation.
-Improve mental health and wellbeing of carers.
-Empower carers to engage with their community
How we do this:
-Provide one-to-one support.
-Run regular drop in ‘social and support’ groups.
-Have experienced and knowledgeable staff on hand to give advice and information.
-Have a helpline manned 10am – 3pm Monday to Friday.
-Work with other local and countywide groups and services.
-Have knowledge of local resources and work with individuals to ‘signpost’ and build individual ‘community maps’

name: Essex- Uniting Friends
description_full:
Our services cover Redbridge, Newham, Havering, Waltham Forest, Barking and Dagenham and Essex.
Our vision is for a world which recognises values and celebrates the lives and equal participation of people with a learning disability in society.
Our purpose is to provide opportunities for people to take part in social and educational activities in order to express their unique identities and develop their skills to reach their full potential in the community.
We are a user-led service, which ensures that adults with a learning disability take a leading role in defining and running their own provision.
See the website for activities such as our holidays, nightclub, allotment, cafe, radio show and more.
https://www.unitingfriends.org.uk/
We always welcome feedback and comments, or simply drop in to us and we'll be happy to see you.

content:
Greenwich Carers Centre provides a ‘One Stop Shop’ service to all carers living within the Borough of Greenwich, or who care for someone living in the Borough of Greenwich. The service provides individual needs-led support via the offering of information, advice, emotional and practical support, representation, advocacy, and consultation opportunities. All specialist knowledge gained from undertaking this work will be used to inform others both locally and nationally, in order to further awareness of Carers issues.

What’s on offer from April 1st:

– a bi-monthly newsletter.
– events with monthly speakers.
– a new Spa for carers which carer for person can also use (charges apply). A lovely dedicated space for carers, tastefully decorated and have some really nice therapies on offer including a Chauffeur to pick up carer!
– The Carers Centre also offer coach day trips, activity sessions, film nights and drop-off respite service (2 hours at the stables £ 8.50 per person per hour plus lunch or refreshment costs).

You can contact the Greenwich Carers Centre by phone, e-mail, post or through their website.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Judith Quin(4): I saw a poster for Greenwich Carers Centre at Oxleas Mental Health services when I was sole carer for someone with severe MH challenges.  I work on my own in my self-employed business and with no partner or family close by or offering the support I needed to support this person ....I was in a wreck of a place.  Simply having people to listen when I first found them was such a blessed relief - to feel heard and seen that my own mental/emotional health was a mess ... and have that acknowledged as understandable and not selfish was much needed.  On top of that - the staff there made sure I was signed up to the specific support group for those caring for people with mental health issues and that I knew about the free events for carers (at the time I joined in on the singing group and yoga a couple of times)  The most amazing thing was being connected with another charity for me to access some much needed counselling  -  Would I have loved more? yes (in-person sessions specifically would have been awesome and maybe not in the middle of the work day)  Could I have made more of it too? yes (I didn't attend the forum - or enough of the yoga)  I had no idea that caring was SO draining on the carer - to have this resource in the borough is an absolute blessing.  Remembering that this is a small charity - they do a BIG job. | Tom Schofield(1): After seeing how this place recommended ABA and PBS, which are proven to cause trauma in Autistic people, I’d already say to avoid this place. However, when educated about this, the uncaring centre not only spoke over Autistic people, they threatened legal action, before fleeing social media like spineless cowards. Disgusting. | Helen Owen(1): The way this organisation treated Emma Dalmayne , a well known and highly respected autistic activist is absolutely disgraceful.  You invited Emma to promote her organisation Autistic Inclusive Meets Community Group Aim. Emma stated what they do and don’t do such as promote ABA and PBS, both of which cause trauma to autistic people, she was then rudely interrupted by another speaker and patronised by one of your staff who tried to shut her down. Emma was then threatened by your CEO who had audacity to threaten legal action which is just plainly condoning the way Emma was treated and is tantamount to bullying. Does that Stuart person really believe Emma, Autistic Inclusive Meets Community Group AIM and all their followers  and supporters are just going to go away? Absolutely no chance, big mistake Greenwich Carers Centre and Stuart.

name: Greenwich Mencap -Activities for Learning Disabilities and ADHD support group   ‘Greenlights’
description_full:
Greenwich Mencap run a range of social activities for people with a learning disability throughout the year. On a monthly basis we run two varying social activities, as well as a weekly lunchtime drop-in where adults with a learning disability can get together and socialise.
Committee Plus, our steering group of adults with a learning disability, also organises events and consultations several times throughout the year.
Activities Page:

Mencap runs a roving ADHD support group in locations around Greenwich on the last Friday of every month. content:
Greenwich Mencap run a range of social activities for people with a learning disability throughout the year. On a monthly basis we run two varying social activities, as well as a weekly lunchtime drop-in where adults with a learning disability can get together and socialise.

We also run a monthly Games night with giant Jenga, picture bingo, a Nintendo Wii, pool, giant connect four, cards and ten-pin bowling.

Committee Plus, our steering group of adults with a learning disability, also organises events and consultations several times throughout the year.

name: Greenwich Toy & Leisure Library
description_full:
Greenwich Toy & Leisure Library Association is a toy library for children with special needs and their families/carers. Established in 1973 the organisation aims to support them by:-
Providing therapeutic, educational, stimulating toys and equipment
Promoting play and recreation in their development
Encouraging the development of self-advocacy skills
Educating parents and carers in the skills and knowledge they need to support and develop their children
Raising the awareness of the development needs of children and young people with disabilities
A toy library of 7,000 items
Home visiting support to parents and carers
Holidays and outings
Visit the Sensory Studio
Help is also offered to small local groups in establishing and running their own toy libraries. There are 19 currently available in the Royal Borough of Greenwich.

full_address: Challengers is an independent children's charity that, for more than 30 years, has developed and delivered inclusive play and leisure for disabled children and young people across Surrey, Hampshire and increasingly the surrounding areas. description_full:
KEEN London is the only charity in London providing one-to-one support at free sports and activity sessions for children with additional needs or disabilities. We believe no child should miss out on the benefits of sports and physical activity. With the support of our amazing volunteers, our weekly sessions give our athletes the chance to play games, make friends and, most importantly, have fun!
At our sessions, each athlete is paired with a trained volunteer coach for individual support. This means they benefit from one-to-one attention and care. It also means their parents can leave their children in a secure and safe environment.
For families, volunteers, supporters and athletes, this means the KEEN family is one of a kind: welcoming, safe, supportive, rewarding and fun.

A user led organisation managed and controlled by disabled people.
Mission:
-To campaign for the rights of disabled people and influence local and national policy and practice.
-To deliver high quality professional accessible activities which promote independent living.
-To give disabled people the knowledge and confidence about their rights enabling them to access opportunity, make choices and live independently.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Ines Vadius (PoemRoseGirl_)(1): Subject: Serious Concerns About Carers from Disability on Action – Please Read  I want to raise a serious complaint and make others aware of my recent experience with carers from Disability on Action, specifically a woman named Dee Dee.  When she first started, she came across as genuine and kind. But shortly after, her whole energy shifted. She became rude—especially toward me—stopped greeting me altogether, and often looked at me with a cold, unfriendly expression. Her behavior made me incredibly uncomfortable in my own home, which is unacceptable from someone in a caregiving role.  What’s even more concerning is that she brought along another woman named Tessie, who wasn’t even from a legitimate care agency. From what I was told, it was Dee Dee who got her involved through personal connections. That’s completely unprofessional and, frankly, dangerous when it comes to vulnerable people needing proper care.  Eventually, they both decided to leave, and honestly—it was for the best. I was holding back from saying something directly, but I feel it’s my duty now to speak up and warn others.  Dee Dee might appear like she cares, but actions speak louder than words. Bringing unqualified friends into care roles, and treating family members with disregard and disrespect, is not only wrong—it’s harmful.  To anyone looking for carers: please be cautious. Bad energy and fake people can slip into these roles, and if you’re not paying attention, they’ll drain your peace of mind and affect your home.  Name them and shame them? Yes—because people like this should not be trusted around vulnerable individuals. Don’t be fooled by fake smiles. | K S(5): Absolutely superb 10/10. Big shout out to Aileen, who is a lovely lady, helpful kind and wonderful person. Helped our family with the forms. Guys please promote and give Aileen a big bonus. She does the service proud. | Suzanne Iwai(4): Difficult to find if a friend had not brought me by car. There is a small parking area that can accommodate 2 or 3 cars. It's behind the main part of Clem Atlee estate. If visiting plan your journey via maps. Fully disabled accessible once inside with generous sized main meeting space for over 30 people. It's an organisation that works hard to support disability rights, independent living, the right social housing to suit your needs and direct service payments from LBHF so you can employ the right level of assistance to do everything already mentioned. Join us, join in and help co produce all the service improvements we wish to see and use.

Hammersmith and Fulham Mencap
http://www.hfmencap.org/
Parent/Carer Advocacy
The Parent/Carer Advocate offers one to one independent support to parents/carers of disabled children aged 0 – 25 resident in Hammersmith & Fulham
Parentsactive is a support network for families of disabled children (0-25) living in the London Borough of Hammersmith and Fulham. The aim of Parentsactive is to enable parents to gain and share information both locally and nationally, make new friends and, most importantly, realise that they are not alone.
Weekly forums are held at the Masbro Children’s Centre, attended by guest speakers from statutory, voluntary and private sectors.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Miha Vieriu(5): My son has been attending weekly for over 2 years. Their input has meant so much to his progress. Always working with dedicated and knowledgeable people. I recommended them to friends and I will keep booking my child in. It’s a great place, exactly what we need. | mia crispin(5): I just wanted to say a big thank you, for everything you have done and continue to do for Kiki. She has been attending Cpotential for just under a year and the progress she has made has been incredible. She has gone from being a child who I never thought would walk to moving around her nursery and playground interacting with her peers. Whilst she still requires assistance with her movement, she is far more independent since attending Cpotential.  She never spoke previously but with the introduction of picture cards, she has started to use phrases to express her feelings and needs and finally said mum for the first time! She loves her speech therapy with Frankie. Occupational therapy has also been amazing and given me so many tools to help regulate Kiki, which has helped her focus more on activities and mealtimes etc. Kiki has been highly motivated to move during music therapy and particularly loves her Aqua therapy sessions with Manthan. Her progress has shocked myself and her teachers and I’m excited to continue her therapies with Cpotential. All the staff are kind and caring and the office staff are brilliant with communication, which as a mum of 2 disabled children really helps me keep on top of appointments! I would highly recommend CPotential | Andrew Pierce(5): I'm so glad CPotential exists. Thank you to everyone who is involved with the running of this charity catering for children with cerebral palsy, my little boy has made great improvements after the 2 week intensive physio course we've done.

name: Haringey -Markfield Project and Parent Partnership Scheme
description_full:
Markfield Road
London
N15 4RB
Call our Reception (Open 9am - 5pm weekdays)
Tel: 0208 800 4134
enquiries@markfield.org.uk
https://markfield.org.uk/
https://markfield.org.uk/haringeys-send-information-advice-support-service/
We run inclusive after school clubs and holiday playschemes where disabled and non-disabled children play together. Markfield’s highly trained staff are experienced in including children with a wide range of disabilities. We have youth clubs in the afternoon and evening with music, art, sport, films, where young people can socialise, relax and choose activities they enjoy. Our daytime and evening social clubs for adults with learning disabilities, autism or Aspergers Syndrome include dance, art, trips and discussion groups.
We have a wide range of family support services running at Markfield and outreach at other local venues. Our services aim to empower families to advocate for their rights, access services and participate in decision making. We focus on ways families can build supportive networks and get involved in the local community. We offer face to face and telephone advice, an information hub, free parent group coffee mornings and a monthly family club. We also community drop in sessions for all local families such as our Saturday Open Access adventure play and Under 5’s stay and play.

name: Harrow Mencap
description_full:
Contact:Julie Browne
Telephone:020 7423 7382
http://www.harrowmencap.org.uk
Description
Harrow Mencap is an independent charity - affiliated to National Mencap.
Started by parents and carers in the late 1940's we became a limited company in 1987. We have grown into a multi-project service provider to support people with learning disabilities and their families. We are proud of our local reputation as the primary campaigning organisation for people with learning disabilities in Harrow.
Our Purpose:-to work with and represent people with learning disabilities and their families and carers to ensure their needs, rights and aspirations are met.
Harrow Mencap's Children's Services offer a variety of play schemes to include Saturdays during term time; 10am-2pm and holiday clubs outside of term time; 10am-3pm weekdays. Saturday clubs operate from our Day Centre at Pinner Green and holiday clubs at Kingsley High School, Whittlesea Road, Harrow, HA3 6ND.
From September, we will also be offering after school clubs at Kinglsey School Harrow, Shaftesbury School Harrow and Woodlands School Edgware, 3.30pm to 5.30pm. We also offer transport as part of the service . Please contact Julie Browne for more information on:
020 8423 7382
email: Julie.browne@harrowmencap.org.uk

description_full:
Our service is free and aimed at families with children who have been diagnosed with autism.
Families First
Supporting families in the home with social, communication, emotional, and behaviour issues through practical support, modelling, training and advice
Ready Steady Go group
6 sessions implementing the Attention Autism Approach with a small group of children and their parents
Play Therapy
1-1 sessions with a qualified Play Therapist
The Chill Out Zone
A 6 week course teaching practical strategies to help manage anxiety
Sibling Support Group - "The Funtastic Fun Club"
Once a month activity group for siblings of children with autism
Sensory Therapy
By a Paediatric Occupational Therapist, either in the home or at a local venue using specialist equipment
Referral Criteria
We accept children aged between 3 and 11 who live within Hart and Rushmoor, including the borders of Surrey, up to a 10 mile radius of Farnborough.
We take referrals directly from parents.

description_full:
Who we are and what we do?
Both these disorders make it difficult for children to succeed in school because of the resulting anxiety and behaviour difficulties. Parents often struggle with the challenging behaviour at home and can find that usual discipline methods are ineffective.
This is where we come in:
At ADD-vance we listen.
At ADD-vance we are non-judgmental.
At ADD-vance we give strategies, support and information.

content:
Who we are and what we do?
ADD-vance is a registered charity set up in Hertfordshire in 1996 to support and empower adults, children and families with Autism, Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) and related conditions in Hertfordshire.
Both these disorders make it difficult for children to succeed in school because of the resulting anxiety and behaviour difficulties. Parents often struggle with the challenging behaviour at home and can find that usual discipline methods are ineffective.
This is where we come in:
At ADD-vance we listen.
At ADD-vance we are non-judgmental.
At ADD-vance we give strategies, support and information.
At ADD-vance we provide a confidential, safe and friendly atmosphere where parents can speak freely (often for the first time) about their child/family issues.
At ADD-vance we give space for emotions.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Jo Tweedy(5): ADD-Vance has been a lifeline for my family. At a time when doors were closed to us, they listened, validated my concerns, and gave the best advice. The knowledge and support I gained from their team and courses have not only helped me cope and fight for my child but also support others along the way. Their QB Test gave us vital evidence that helped secure an EHCP, and my child is now thriving because of it. It has also helped us be taken seriously to be considered for diagnosis which was being constantly dismissed by professionals despite the severity of my child’s struggles to access their education. I cannot thank Georgia, Lisa, Amanda, Sophie, and the whole team enough — you’ve carried us through some of our darkest moments and celebrated the brighter ones with us. Feels nice to be out the other side of the storm thanks to ADD-Vance. There will always be something to fight for but we are so blessed to have this service I owe them my sanity and can’t thank them enough. ADD-Vance truly is vital for SEN parents, and services like this are essential to ensure families are heard, supported, and able to help their children flourish. | Georgina Barrell(5): Absolutely incredible support from the most incredible team. I actually do not know what I would have done without their support. The training sessions, helpline and 1:1 coaching have all been incredibly helpful and quite honestly, these guys just GET IT. They know where you’re at and they have real lived in experience of how to help. A special shout out to Talia who has been a literal guardian angel over the past few months and has supported us, making a huge difference not only for my daughter but for our family as a whole. I could not recommend highly enough. | Roisin K(5): Such a wonderful charity, the staff are very supportive and there are lots of options for support, plus the online training and workshops are really helpful resources.  The lending space is also brilliant for families looking to try out different equipment and toys. Thank you!

gmaps_reviews_top3: Lilly M(5): Really helpful, lovely staff and so much information available. | Dan Gilberthorpe(5): This is a friendly place staffed by knowledgeable volunteers and offering great courses and support for autism and ADHD. The information provided is brilliant and the focus is very much on the real world. All the volunteers have personal experience as well as academic and clinical knowledge so the support offered is human, kind and effective. I can't praise or thank them enough for the help they've been to my whole family. Amazing people. | Jacqueline Foxe(5): The Centre for ADHD and Autism support is a hidden gem as far as I'm concerned. I walked in on their open session and received a warm welcome. I had someone sit with me and get to know our family's story. I had a coffee whilst we chatted about where I needed help and advice. The staff were extremely knowledgeable and were able to explain why I was experiencing problems getting help from our school and local authority. They run classes on living with and understanding ADHD and autism but these sell out fast and as yet I've not managed to get on one. A great place to drop in and have a chat with others in the same position as yourself.

name: Islington- Palace For All
description_full:
Palace for All provides services primarily for children with physical and or learning disabilities to enable them to reach their full potential.
Stay and Play
Palace has a large play space with lots of toys, craft activities, books and musical instruments. The play leader encourages constructive play and leads story time, drama sessions, singing and crafts. There is also plenty of opportunity for free play.
Therapies
Palace is a centre where children can get the therapies they need and where parents and carers can get support and relax in a friendly environment, learn from each other’s experiences and have a chat over a cup of tea.
These include:-
-Music Therapy
-Social Communication Sessions
-Speech and Language Therapy
-Cranial Osteopathy
-Reflexology
Contact:-
http://palaceforall.org.uk
Tel: 020 7561 1689
email: info@palaceforall.org.uk
Address:-
The Laundry,
Sparsholt Road,
London N19 4EL

name: Kingston- Express CIC
description_full:
Express is a user-led organisation, self-referrals are welcomed and our support services have an open-door policy. Express reach over 1000 families, providing information, parent support groups, therapeutic groups, counselling services for parent/carers and a specific specialist counselling service for autistic young adults. Express also work with local universities and colleges to provide placements for dramatherapy, psychology and counselling students.
Express has now grown significantly to be the ‘go to’ place for families across the community. The long-term vision is to create a social environment where autistic young people and their families feel welcomed and secure; a place where young people can gain valuable social skills and work experience. A place for everyone.
To join our services please sign up to our eNewsletter and contact us www.expresscic.org.uk/contact-us to request our registration form.
Our facebook page also has lots of information including a link to join our closed forum for additional peer support.
Best wishes,
Annette & Tracey
Website www.expresscic.org.uk
Twitter @expresscic Facebook @expresscic

Express is a true force for community cohesion - reducing the risk of isolation and vulnerability among autistic children, young people and their families through personalised support, group networking and events which bring the community together.
city: group networking and events which bring the community together.
content:
Based in Kingston upon Thames, Express CIC is a non-profit community interest company founded in September 2012 to help support autistic young people and to promote awareness, understanding and acceptance in the community. Express is a true force for community cohesion - reducing the risk of isolation and vulnerability among autistic children, young people and their families through personalised support, group networking and events which bring the community together. 

Express is a user-led organisation, self-referrals are welcomed and our support services have an open-door policy. Express reach over 1000 families, providing information, parent support groups, therapeutic groups, counselling services for parent/carers and a specific specialist counselling service for autistic young adults. Express also work with local universities and colleges to provide placements for dramatherapy, psychology and counselling students. 

Express has now grown significantly to be the ‘go to’ place for families across the community. The long-term vision is to create a social environment where autistic young people and their families feel welcomed and secure; a place where young people can gain valuable social skills and work experience. A place for everyone. 

To join our services please sign up to our eNewsletter and contact us www.expresscic.org.uk/contact-us to request our registration form. 

Our facebook page also has lots of information including a link to join our closed forum for additional peer support. 

Best wishes, 
Annette & Tracey 

Website www.expresscic.org.uk 
Twitter @expresscic Facebook @expresscic 
Donate www.localgiving.org/donation/expresscic

 Early Bird Courses
description_full:
Drumbeat Outreach
Brockley Site
London
SE4 2PR
Tel: 0745 0041642
Email: s.gannon@drumbeat.lewisham.sch.uk
Drumbeat Outreach is a team of teachers and specialist advisers offering support, advice and training to children, families and professionals in Lewisham. We work across all mainstream educational settings, including Early Years, Primary and Secondary schools, and Post Sixteen provisions. Schools and Colleges can buy in support from the Outreach Team through a Service Level Agreement (SLA).
We run three licensed Earlybird, Earlybird Plus and Cygnet courses for the parents of children newly diagnosed on the autism spectrum. These courses run over a number of weeks and are facilitated by multi-disciplinary teams within Drumbeat and in collaboration with our partners in health.
We also run parent workshops and drop in groups across the school year in schools, children’s centres and at Drumbeat.

name:
Lewisham- SIGNAL 
(Support, Information, Guidance & News for Autism in Lewisham)
description_full:
Signal Family Support, C/o Hatcham Oak Children’s Centre
Brockley
London
SE4 2NX
Tel: 07946 632477
http://www.signalfamilysupport.org
SIGNAL (Support, Information, Guidance & News for Autism in Lewisham) is a local parent support group, based in the London Borough of Lewisham, set up a number of years ago by parents of children who have autism, Asperger syndrome or other autism spectrum disorders (ASDs).
The group hold monthly meetings which include talks from experts on a variety of topics related to autism, including current research and educational provision.
Coffee mornings, held in different members houses, are open to all members to exchange ideas, experiences and information or just have a chat.
The group produces a quarterly newsletter and runs a borrowing library with many specialist books, journals and articles, videos, and specially selected children’s books. It maintains links with local schools, hold conferences for schools and teachers, organises drama therapy and trampolining activities, provides parent speakers for local functions to raise autism awareness and fundraises within the local community and through grants.
It also runs a 16 plus and a sibling support club, has an annual party, and runs and subsidises holiday outings and other activities for its membership.

We provide impartial advice and information on education issues to parents/carers of children who experience barriers to learning such as special educational needs (SEN), disability, exclusion or bullying. What do we do?: We can attend and support parents/carers in meetings about their child’s education, and offer support if the parent/carer wishes to request a statutory assessment. We also help with writing letters and reports. Who is it for?: Parents/carers of children and young people who are attending school in Newham, or who attend an out of borough school if the child is a Newham resident. What age group does it cover?: We have a statutory responsibility to work with children and young people up to 25 years for those who have a Statement of Special Educational Need.

gmaps_reviews_top3: DMR_1(1): Any struggling carer knows that the one organisation you will be directed to is this one. Where does all the money they get from so many big funders and the government go? Because all they do to "support" carers is throw us £600, once a year. The rest, I can only assume, is used to line their pockets. I know for a fact that they are not all volunteers. A glassdoor review from an employee specifically mentioned "great perks".  Several years on, and all I get is an annual assesment, which grants me £600. I have called countless times saying that I desperately need support more than money (I am a full-time, unpayed carer for both my disabled parents, and a full-time student). They, especially Christopher, say they will help and I never hear anything back. When I follow up, they are sometimes even sarcastic with me and pretend to apologise for not being able to help.  Their website, under "what we do" claims to have helped one woman set up power pf attorney.  This is their one one and only testimony. I asked them for help with this same process four years ago, and they refused to even advise. I'm completely fed up with this fraud of a charity.  Carers have one of the hardest jobs on the planet, and they spit in our faces. | ad moh(1): Very bad service regarding keeping up with so called backlog of personal assessments.. speed it up.. your not upholding from contract from local authorities | K. content:
Aut-Haslemere is a support group in Haslemere, run BY and FOR autistic/Asperger adults aged 18 or over. 

We meet monthly on the first Monday of the month to discuss the issues that affect us, and share tips and strategies for living the best autistic lives possible. You do not need a formal diagnosis to attend

You can choose whether and how much you participate in the discussion, by speaking or in writing, or just listen.

Light refreshments will be provided during the break, when you can chat to others if you choose.

We are asking for a £2 suggested donation to help cover costs.

Like this page to see updates about Aut-Haslemere.

If you would like to attend, please make contact via email, phone, text message, Facebook message, or mail. We speak to new members first to confirm the group is suitable.

gmaps_reviews_top3: DancingGaye(2): Normally I call and leave a message as i can't access e mail or see small screens. My hands also don't navigate online. The organisation would do well to have an anticipatory communication process rather than spend hours trying to debate or discuss my comms needs each time - to be met with negative language about diverse needs - very stressful and invasive. I've spent weeks and months explaining myself over and over. I called another organisation - in 5 mins they offered me Telephone form filling with a scribe, no more than 1 question at a time, meetings face to face with a list for ND people - voice memos - with the same info in writing - questions in 1 liner 3's in bullet points or alpha or numerical order. The point is not only does it enable a range of disabled or comms needs before they escalate but it saves so much time in the long run. No need for repeating needs each time.  The male receptionist who answered to day was not helpful, repeated line by line like a script what he could not do several times. Then carried on a conversation when he should know working in disability when a person is upset and has cognitive stress as part of a cognitive condition. He didn't.  That is why "i find it difficult on the phone" as was remarked lime it is my probelm not the communicator  - it is Not due to my communication not being nuero typical or disability - it is because the phone communications are not nuero divergent accessible due to the reception being divisive - anti autism and not aware of invisible disabilites  - this fits in cognitive disabled friendly or accessibility at RUILS. Life is hard enough as it is without more of the same nuero typical ways of working. If RNID, RNIB and Scope could do it in the 90's when I was a learning and comms trainer there, then why is it such a problem now ? | MoIdris Malik(5): Excellent staff at Richmond AID they have activities going on a food bank and they helped with my PIP highly recommend them. Peer support workers were amazing too. | Ronnie McDowall(4): Very helpful team of people, beware that parking is very tight though.

name:

(Special Educational Needs and Disabilities Information Advice and Support Service)
description_full:
Richmond and Kingston Special Educational Needs and Disabilities Information Advice and Support Service or SENDIASS is a free, confidential and impartial service for parents and carers, children and young people (up to 25 years).
Richmond,
London,
TW92HP
Tel: 0208 831 6179
email: RichmondKingston@kids.org.uk
https://www.kids.org.uk/richmond-and-kingston-sendiass
The type and range of support offered includes:
-Information regarding the local offer, local policy and SEN/ disability law from independently trained staff.
-Advice for parent/carers, children and young people on gathering, understanding and interpreting information and applying it to their own situation.
-Personalisation of personal budgets
Information on the local authority’s processes for resolving disagreements, its complaints procedures and means of redress.
-Signposting children, young people and parents to alternative and additional sources of local and national information, advice and support.
Individual casework and representation where needed including support in attending meetings, contributing to assessments and reviews and participating in decisions about outcomes for the child or young person.
-Support for parents and young people in managing mediation and appeals to the SEND Tribunal.

name:

Choice Support
description_full:
Who do we support?
You must have funding in place to pay for the support. This could be funding through the London Borough of Bexley, through a personal budget or your own money.
How do I join the Ken Boyce Centre?
You can self-refer to us - just drop us an email or call us. You can get referred through social services at the London Borough of Bexley.
You may wish to visit us before you join. We'll give you a tour around the building and you'll get to meet the manager and the staff.
Parents group
Join our group for parents. We meet four times a year at the Ken Boyce Centre.
This is your opportunity to be more involved in what we do and be part of something special. You’ll be able to share advice, find out what’s going on and help organise fundraising events.

full_address: Special Voices is a group of parents/carers of children and young people with special/additional needs. We are all volunteers working in partnership with voluntary and statutory organisations. The main aim of Special Voices is to raise awareness about the rights and needs of children/young people with special needs and to ensure that they and their families are consulted and involved in any decisions made during planning or developing services for them. Special Voices has a main steering group of voluntary parents/carers who have taken on the role of gathering your collective views and presenting them to service planners and decision makers in order to ensure that services are needs-led.

name:
South Bucks
 Rainbow Social Club
Bourne End
Westfield School
description_full:
This is a club that aims to enhance social interactions and well-being for children with autism. Meets at Westfield School on Monday evenings 5.00-6.45pm, term time only.
Suitable for children on the autism spectrum and their siblings; up to the age of 11.
We pride ourselves on offering a friendly environment for children and their families.
We aim to offer a wide variety of activities to promote positive interactions, self-esteem and well-being.
Activities include board games, arts & crafts, toys including trains sets, PE equipment in hall and many more things.
An opportunity to meet other parents and have a coffee.
Suggested voluntary donation of £4.00 per family includes a drink and a snack.
For more information, or to come along to the club, please contact:
Heather Wildsmith: Heather.Wildsmith@nas.org.uk
Westfield School,
Highfield Road,
Bourne End,
SL8 5BE

The activities will include Indoor chill out and getting to know you sessions:
•Art •Drama •Cookery •Music •Games
Outings to places our young people choose such as •Theatres •Swimming •Local Sports Centres

There is a charge for this service. Subsidised places are available for those on low income.

gmaps_reviews_top3: TOM ALO WM(1): My experience with Resources for Autism has unfortunately been extremely disappointing and frustrating. From the outset, the service has felt chaotic and poorly managed.  Firstly, the communication system is unnecessarily confusing. Having two separate phone numbers for London and Birmingham makes little sense. A single phone line with clear menu options would be far more effective. When I called, the staff member I spoke to seemed uninterested, unwilling to answer questions, and simply tried to pass me on. Staff should be trained and equipped with knowledge about both branches so that they can provide consistent, accurate information. Instead, I was left feeling dismissed and unsupported.  When I raised my concerns, I was told to email my details so someone could call me back. I waited over a month with no response. Only after I chased it up did anyone acknowledge my email. Even then, I was told that I shouldn’t have been promised a call in the first place—something I found very unprofessional. If a service promises to follow up, it should follow through. Ignoring emails—especially when they outline personal struggles and support needs—is unacceptable. A simple acknowledgment message or short response would have been far better than silence.  The overall handling of my case has been poor: •	Promises were made that were not kept. •	My email was ignored until I chased it. •	Staff appeared untrained, disinterested, and inconsistent in their information. •	Communication across the organisation seemed disjointed, from reception to admin to management.  I had real hopes that Resources for Autism could provide meaningful support. Instead, I felt let down, fobbed off, and left questioning whether an organisation that cannot manage basic communication is truly autism-friendly.  What should have happened is simple: someone should have explained what services are available, clarified the referral process, and set realistic expectations. That would have gone a long way to building trust. Instead, the lack of professionalism and broken promises have completely undermined my confidence in the service.  I strongly urge Resources for Autism to reflect on: •	Communication processes (including phone lines and email responses). •	Staff training, ensuring all staff can provide clear and consistent information. •	Transparency about services offered, without overpromising or backtracking. •	Building an approach that is genuinely autism-friendly, which means clarity, consistency, and reliability.  At present, my experience has been so negative that I would not recommend Resources for Autism. Unless these serious issues are addressed, others will likely feel the same disappointment and frustration that I did. | Michelle(1): I reached out in february, because I felt the signs of an approaching autistic burnout. I was hoping to learn any copying mechanisms, or at least receive some sort of support / someone to talk to without judgement, which is according to their website something they take pride in.  I had a phone call with someone who said they'll put me on the waiting list for therapy, and they'll send me some helpful information.  The information provided was unfortunately hardly of any use, and I don't think they put me on the waiting list at all. They told me they'd get back to me in October for therapy. It is December as I am writing this and I still haven't heard back from them, even after reaching out per email last month...  In the end, I did burn out, lost my job, and now struggle to find a new one, which is exactly what I wanted to avoid by reaching out, so thanks for nothing! | Sa ku Ra(5): Their staff are all well trained and friendly.  My son had a great experience with them.

name: Southwark -Salmon Youth Centre
description_full:
Contact Matt Perkins for details,
Tel:  020 7237 3788
Email: matt.perkins@salmonyouthcentre.org
43 Old Jamaica Rd,  Bermondsey, Southwark.
London SE16 4TE,
DISABLED YOUNG PEOPLE
We want to make the Salmon Youth Centre a place where all young people can come and enjoy themselves – even if they have a disability that they think might make this difficult.
CLUBS
To start with, we have a specific Youth club for young people who have a disability and are aged 14+ (Friday, 3.30-5.30pm). In the club we do a range of activities (e.g. sport, games, drama, music, computers) and projects (e.g. making a DVD and volunteering at the Bermondsey Carnival).
As well as the clubs specifically for young people who have a disability, our whole programme of activities are available to you (see programme). Many of our staff have experience of working with young people who have a disability and we can arrange extra support to access sessions if you need it. We are also hoping to set up a parent and carer’s room on Saturday nights to support young people who have a disability to access the general 14+ session that happens between 8-10pm.
RESIDENTIALS AND TRIPS
We go on trips to places like Thorpe Park and 10 pin bowling. This year we are hoping to go on our first two summer residential holidays which are specifically for young people who have a disability.
NEW SCHEME
Connect2 is a new scheme which pairs up young people who have a disability with trained volunteers who take them out into the local community. As well as having fun and getting out and about, it is hoped that this scheme will help improve young people’s confidence and independence skills.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Tessa Williams(5): We accidentally stumbled across The Arts House in the Summer of 2024 when Joe and the team were on Sutton High Street offering free trial sessions when the school was in its infancy.  My daughter is obsessed with performing and having had not so great experiences at other schools (due to a lack of understanding of my daughters needs at the time) I was wary of taking up the trial session. Nevertheless we took the offer and I am so glad we did.  My little lady has excelled in her technique in all areas (Dance and Drama being her strong suits) and her confidence in herself and her abilities has exploded!! I’ve even had comments from her teachers at school say they have seen a change in her personality (for the good)  To Joe, Olivia, Phoebe, Rachel and the rest of the team.  The Arts House isn’t a business, it’s a safe space for the students to be themselves, to express and explore their love of performing among other like minded children who can do exactly the same. They make it feel like a family.  My daughter loves going to her classes every week and it’s so obvious from how happy the children are on arrival and collection that everyone is having a great time.  This evening was the end of year performance and it was so obvious the amount of hard work that had gone into it. Not by just the children but the adults to. It ran like a well oiled machine and I can’t wait to see what this next year will bring us!  So congratulations to all involved in making The Arts House the phenomenal success that it is fast becoming! You should all be very proud!! | Meliha Mehmedović-Wong(5): We couldn’t be happier with our experience at the Arts House. From the very beginning, it was clear that this is more than just a place for drama, singing, and dancing lessons—it’s a true community. The team’s dedication, passion, and support have had such a positive impact on our daughter. She’s grown in confidence, developed her talents, and most importantly, found a place where she truly belongs.  The environment feels like a close-knit family. gmaps_reviews_top3: Penny Morrissey(5): I cannot recommend SEN talk highly enough. It really has been a lifeline since being introduced to the service a year ago. Annaliese, the director and founder is always there to listen and offers helpful, informative and supportive advice. She truly understands the challenges faced by families with children with additional needs and is always ready to help in any way she can.  The Lego therapy sessions my younger son attends has really helped improve his social communication skills and the support and help annaliese has given my older son whilst he has had difficulties at school has been invaluable.  This review really doesn't do SEN talk justice and if I could give 10 stars I would!! Amazing organisation! | Aisling Cahill(5): When my son was diagnosed with ADHD and ASD last year I was completely overwhelmed and quickly found that there was not much support out there for my son, especially as he was high functioning. There was very little in my area that offered advice or services. After being told about everything Annaliese had created at Sen Talk I was instantly reassured and at ease after our first conversation. It is absolutely amazing what she has accomplished and provides for children and families that have children with SEN needs. I was fortunate to complete a parent course to help understand Sensory Issues and this has helped me immensely with helping my son on a day to day basis and understanding how his environment affects him and ways to reduce this. He also attends the Lego Club and really enjoys this and I can already see it has helped with his socialising skills. He is excited to turn 7 and join the communicate youth group head on a Friday and to go on his first trip with Sen Talk to godstone farm.  They also have mont coffee mornings and evening sessions where they have speakers for all difference specialist areas like  EHCPs, school exclusions, occupational therapist and more.  Sen Talk has been a lifeline for me and I know it is to so many children and families and I would highly recommend the services to everyone. It has been great to meet other parents that are having the same experiences as me and understand what you’re going through.  Thank you Sen Talk. Keep up the amazing work!!! | Kambala Chair(4): I have watched Sen Talk CIC grow into a fantastic resource for parents, carers and even Grandparents of children that are Autistic. Annaliese will go above and beyond to make sure that all her families get exactly what they need to help them on their fabulous journey, I say fabulous because it is a very special journey and with the right tools to meet emotional and physical need it becomes a little easier. I am Noah's Grandmother and had no idea how to deal with what I thought behaviour problems. I am cringing  writing this :-( I have learnt such a lot been on many courses and seen amazing changes in the children and parents that have attended group sessions, training and especially Lego based therapy... Amazing charity, keep up the good work !

gmaps_reviews_top3: Mike(1): Awful: I booked this for the Easter Holidays as it said it was "Chance to learn new skills and meet new friends “Be coached by Watford’s award-winning Trust team” "Daily prizes to be won" "Participants will be coached by Watford FC CSE Trust coaches " First day, I got the times mixed up, thinking it finished at 4pm instead of 3pm I turned up to pick my son up at 3.55pm, he was the only one there, as stated it had finished at 3pm,I asked if they had my number and they said yes? As he left, nobody said “thank you” or “well done”  When he got home he told me he was upset and worried ( so much for safe guarding) they got split up from all their friends and more importantly for those that had signed up for this course, there was no Coaching or learning new skills! Instead they just played world cup football non-stop for the whole three days.  A couple of times when I turned up, FOUR teams were playing with no supervision, another time Four teams were playing with either just One coach or Two at the most, just watching and blowing the whistle at half time and full time, no interaction with the children’s what so ever of the games I was watching. When I emailed and complained I did not get an answer but the following morning the Head Coach who I presume was running (the courses) asked to have a word, not as you may off thought to apologise, his response to my complaint was. I arrived at the club on a valid guest pass, invited by my brother, a member who was running a few minutes late. Despite explaining the situation, I was spoken to by the front desk staff in a rude and patronising manner. They refused to let me inside and insisted I wait awkwardly at the entrance after I had gone in through the barrier already using my guess pass , even though my sister-in-law—also a member—was already inside the club. Shockingly, they insisted that she physically come out to collect me, as if I were a child or security risk.  I completely understand that clubs have rules, but what was missing here was simple human decency, flexibility, and any semblance of customer service. I was left standing there while members walked by, feeling embarrassed and publicly singled out for no valid reason. All it would have taken was a little empathy or discretion—perhaps allowing me to wait in the lobby for a few minutes until my brother arrived.  This experience made me feel not only unwelcome but also judged and belittled. The rigid and dismissive attitude of the staff is unacceptable, especially in a place that markets itself as a premium health and leisure club.  I urge David Lloyd to review how their staff are trained to deal with guest scenarios and consider the importance of discretion and dignity in customer interactions. gmaps_reviews_top3: DMR_1(1): Any struggling carer knows that the one organisation you will be directed to is this one. Where does all the money they get from so many big funders and the government go? Because all they do to "support" carers is throw us £600, once a year. The rest, I can only assume, is used to line their pockets. I know for a fact that they are not all volunteers. A glassdoor review from an employee specifically mentioned "great perks".  Several years on, and all I get is an annual assesment, which grants me £600. I have called countless times saying that I desperately need support more than money (I am a full-time, unpayed carer for both my disabled parents, and a full-time student). They, especially Christopher, say they will help and I never hear anything back. When I follow up, they are sometimes even sarcastic with me and pretend to apologise for not being able to help. name:
Westminster- 
In-Deep Children’s Music Therapy
description_full:
The sessions will be supported by a group of trained volunteers and are run by a qualified, experienced therapist.
Alongside the sessions, In-Deep will be providing parents a place where they can meet and share their experiences with other parents and carers; as many parents who care for a child with special needs and/or disabilities can often find themselves feeling isolated, overwhelmed and alone.
Music therapy has long been shown to help children communicate and help develop speech and further physical, emotional and mental development.
If your child has learning difficulties or disabilities just bring them along to one of our sessions all children will be welcomed and catered for. For more information contact Emma Chapman by using the email:
emma.chapman@in-deep.org.uk or by calling: tel: 07761263105 or 02078345204.

Alongside the sessions, In-Deep will be providing parents a place where they can meet and share their experiences with other parents and carers; as many parents who care for a child with special needs and/or disabilities can often find themselves feeling isolated, overwhelmed and alone.

Music therapy has long been shown to help children communicate and help develop speech and further physical, emotional and mental development.
If your child has learning difficulties or disabilities just bring them along to one of our sessions all children will be welcomed and catered for. For more information contact Emma Chapman by using the email:

name: Westminster Parents Participation Group
description_full:
We are a group of parent carer volunteers working together as Westminster Parent Participation Group (WPPG). This parent led group is for all Westminster parent and carers of children and adults with special educational needs and/or a disability (up to 25 years).
-We create a space at our drop-in sessions where parents can meet together, ask questions, talk and feel listened to.
-We can help you to access information, services and support.
-We run weekly drop-ins, information sessions and consultation events (term time only)
We meet every Thursday during school term time at our LDN living office between 10am-12pm. LDN Drop-in 389a Harrow Road, W9 3NA
We now also meet on Tuesdays between 10am -12pm at the Victoria Medical Centre, 29 Upper Tachbrook Street, SW1V 1SN
You are free to drop–in and meet parents/carers just like you and share your stories with them. We can also help guide you in the right direction to finding the right people to help you further.

name: Westminster Tresham Play Service - After School/Youth Club
description_full:
Tresham is a new purpose built play centre on the Lisson Green estate and play places are provided to support children with disabilities to be able to play, develop and have fun alongside local mainstream children accessing the service.
Tresham has a safe outdoor back area, a large multi purpose sports hall and a versatile play space offering art and craft, cooking, music and other creative play opportunities. The building has security to ensure that entering and exiting is closely monitored.
There is a team of staff available on site at all times and staff are experienced in and trained to understand and meet the needs of disabled children.

name: Kent Autistic Trust - Living Services
description_full:
Living Services
Some of our homes are Registered with the Care Quality Commission and regular inspection reports are available on the Care Quality Commission website.
We also provide support for people from self-contained flats in shared accommodation with communal areas with the levels of staff determined by the individuals who live there. Staff can be shared to enable a higher overall level of support. This type of support is especially cost effective and flexible for people who want less support.
For people who are supported through Supporting People funding we have Steady State Contracts with Local Authorities and are required to adhere to a Quality Assessment Framework.
However a person chooses to get their support from the Trust we believe in an individual, personal approach. Providing sensitive support at the level determined by each individual’s need. We use person centred approaches and work with each person to find the right balance of positive aid for maximum independence, choice and determination. We believe that each person can tell us what they want in their own home and we work hard to ensure that we have all the mechanisms in place to make sure we hear what people tell us whatever their method of communication.
For more detailed information about our support services please visit this section of our website:
https://www.kentautistic.com/what-we-do/living-services/
Contact details:
Tel: 01634 405168
email: office@kentautistic.com
full_address: The Trust offers all year round living support. We can provide this in people’s own or family homes and within single or shared accommodations. We are able to provide rented single person and shared accommodation, within small homes for up to six people.

content:
Living Services
The Trust offers all year round living support. We can provide this in people’s own or family homes and within single or shared accommodations. We are able to provide rented single person and shared accommodation, within small homes for up to six people.

For people who are supported through Supporting People funding we have Steady State Contracts with Local Authorities and are required to adhere to a Quality Assessment Framework.

However a person chooses to get their support from the Trust we believe in an individual, personal approach. Providing sensitive support at the level determined by each individual’s need. We use person centred approaches and work with each person to find the right balance of positive aid for maximum independence, choice and determination. We believe that each person can tell us what they want in their own home and we work hard to ensure that we have all the mechanisms in place to make sure we hear what people tell us whatever their method of communication.

For more detailed information about our support services please visit this section of our website:

gmaps_reviews_top3: Christopher J Keeffe(5): The Jewish museum is a hidden gem of a museum. It is important to check the museum website for opening times and tickets etc. As a non Jew I found the museum a treasure trove of Jewish life and history on this island. Of interest is the exhibition of the lived experience of Judaism in the UK. And the important contribution of Jews to British life. An essential part of the solero of British life history. The Holocaust is narrated through the eyes of a British Jew who found himself and his wife caught up in the madness of antisemitism. His testimony is moving as are the artifacts of life in these camps. A visit is very recommended. | Miles Macdonald(5): I really enjoyed my visit here and learnt alot about the history and culture of Jews in Britain from the first settlers who came over in 1066. The entry price is very fair, £8.50 Adult and £6.50 concession. The museum itself has three floors each with a different aspect of Jewish culture. The top floor is currently dedicated to the artistic work of Charlotte Salomon describing her life through a chronological set of paintings. The 2nd floor is devoted to the history and culture associated with Jewish settlement in England and is the core of this museum. It also houses a section on the holocaust. On the first floor you have all the beautiful Jewish religious artifacts, many focused on the Torah, the holy book. | Snir Seroussi(4): this museum is a nice place for people who wants to learn about the jews. as a jew,it was nice to see  the exhibition ,but the information wasn't much useful. History of jews in Britain was nice,but the 3rd floor exhibition of pictures that was when i was there (this floor seems to change from time to time) was boring for me at least. There is a class there,suitable for schools or universities that want to learn a bit about Judaism, but Judaism is much more than showed there...
gmaps_editorial_summary: Modern museum with a re-created Victorian Jewish Quarter, historical artefacts & Holocaust tales.
