gmaps_reviews_top3: Abdullah Riaz(4): It's a very good spot to visit with kids, especially if you want to learn about British history as well. There are sections of the natural museum, Marconi inventions, the Essex army regiment, and a drawing and history area of prehistoric, Anglo-Saxon, Roman, and modern sections. Half a day is enough. There is a park in front for a picnic and a play area at the back for children. There is a cafe on site. | Mel Collins(5): Great place to visit. Super friendly staff. It is a tad small but it’s got lovely things to see. It was nice and quiet when we visited. Definitely would recommend getting there early to get a car spot in the car park. There is a toilet at the car park and in the museum | Sadek Miah(4): A delightful museum where the focus on the Roman presence in Chelmsford makes up quite an interesting part of the place. Chelmsford also being the birthplace of radio means there's a fascinating learning space to understand and digest said information and it's hugely informative. Other exhibits focus on the city's role during the war (the mannequins here are excellent) as well as educational films about how Chelmsford came to be - do look out for the room detailing the complete timeline as well as snippets of facts about this storied city as well as the notable characters that shaped it all. Plenty for the kids to get stuck into and all of it is free! Would definitely recommend.
gmaps_editorial_summary: Free-admission museum in a Victorian house & modern extension, dedicated to local history & culture.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Lola Dines!(4): It was my first time at this museum and I really enjoyed my visit. It is quite small but has a very good selection of historical artifacts. The world section was good. The music room was incredible. There were so many different instruments displayed. You don't have to pay to enter the museum but there sections you need a paid ticket to view like the aquarium and the butterfly garden or greenhouse. I only visited the aquarium on this visit. I bought a ticket from the reception, it was only £6 which was good. The aquarium was in the basement floor and was nice. They had a good selection of sea life, that were amazing to see. I think the last time for entry on both the butterfly house and the aquarium is 4 or 4:30 pm. I didn't make it to the zoo and did not even realise they had it until later. Hopefully I'll see it next time. I loved the gardens and I'm glad I came during the summer to experience it's beauty. Overall it was a great way to spend the afternoon. I would visit again. | Tony(5): This museum and garden in Forest Hill, South London, is free and family-friendly.  The garden features many benches and shaded areas, making it a great place to relax. There is also a beautiful sunken garden that invites exploration and leisurely strolls.  You can find exhibitions on musical instruments, natural history, and anthropology. Additionally, there is a butterfly house and a small aquarium that visitors can enjoy for a fee.  Highly recommend visiting here | David Wright(5): What a great museum! Lots of interesting items from all over the world, good descriptions and explanations of everything too. We went in holiday time and there were a lot of kids running around but there's plenty of space inside and outside. The cafe is excellent too. We could have spent the entire day here, the museum, not just the cafe!
gmaps_editorial_summary: Museum with aquarium, stuffed animal and musical instrument collections, set in landscaped gardens.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Supreme intelligence Bureau(5): The London Transport Museum is an absolute delight for all ages! It beautifully showcases how London’s transport system evolved — from horse-drawn carriages to iconic red buses and the Underground. The exhibits are interactive, informative, and very well presented. The vintage vehicles and classic Tube designs bring back nostalgia, while the hands-on sections make it perfect for kids too. A must-visit for anyone curious about London’s past and how its world-famous transport network came to life! | Emran Hossain(4): History & revolution of London transport system. Kids friendly museum they will love this place. It has also the history of transport system how they developed year after year from horse riding car to automated car now. Kids have a opportunity to make fun & play. Restaurants, shopping market, street funshow available here. The nearest train station is Leister for piccadilly line next to china town. | Jessica Mitchell(4): Such an interesting museum. Includes all different transport methods and has been made for anyone who has an interest in transport. I went on my own as an adult and really enjoyed it. Staff super friendly and helpful. Also £25 to access for a year is very reasonable. Only bit of advice, don’t go in summer holidays, there were lots of children running around with parents that didn’t care which dampened my experience but I know that the museum can’t help this.
gmaps_editorial_summary: World's leading museum of urban transport with galleries of vintage London trams, trains & buses.

gmaps_reviews_top3: The Tewkes(3): There is no clear flow to some parts of the museum & I think like it would be really easy to miss bits. The staff were friendly & they had some good pieces in there. They could do with making it a bit interactive for kids. Took a couple of hours to walk though it all. | A. O(5): It's free. So that's a bonus. You can make a donation. They ask for £5 and it's worth it. It was great to learn more about the city that I've lived in for nearly my whole life. I've never been before. I should've gone before. It's not just for tourists. I was enticed to come by the Harry Kane exhibit and I loved it. It was amazing to see all the other exhibits that they have. Would highly recommend it. The place has moody lighting. It's not a bright museum. Actually I would say it's dark inside. | B Hos(5): London has some of the best museums in the world and so many of then are free to boot. This museum is well set up to tell the story of of London from prehistoric times all the way into the modern Era. Each section highlights major events, technologies, and traditions to illustrate the corresponding time period. The medieval section and the women's suffrage exhibits particularly captured my attention.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Fox Steve(5): I've been to RA several times. This time, I'd like to share some of my feelings about the new exhibition and my evaluation of this location. First of all, I want to praise the staff at the front desk. A lady greeted me with a very gentle and warm smile when I was buying a ticket, which made the entire experience start on a good note. Then we needed to walk through a long exhibition area to another building to see the exhibition. The content of the exhibition was actually not much, and from multiple perspectives, I think Van Gogh's works are far superior to the other ones. The store area after the exhibition was very pleasant to browse, and the design of the souvenirs was very interesting. | James Keeler(4): Visiting the Royal Academy of Arts in London was a deeply enriching experience, marked by standout exhibitions that left a lasting impression. The Kerry James Marshall retrospective was a profound journey through his dynamic exploration of Black identity and history, showcasing his powerful storytelling through vivid, captivating works. Equally compelling was the joint exhibition featuring Anselm Kiefer and Van Gogh, where Kiefer’s monumental, texture-rich paintings contrasted beautifully with Van Gogh’s emotive brushwork, creating a striking dialogue between the two masters across time. The visit was not only visually stunning but intellectually stimulating, offering a rare opportunity to witness the convergence of contemporary and classic artistic genius in one remarkable institution. | Andrew Garcia(5): Great place to pop in and visit. They have areas free of charge as well as pay areas. The Royal Academy of Arts (RA) is an art institution based in Burlington House in Piccadilly London, England. Founded in 1768, it has a unique position as an independent, privately funded institution led by eminent artists and architects. Its purpose is to promote the creation, enjoyment and appreciation of the fine arts through exhibitions, education and debate.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Yulia Voevodskaya(5): Very inspiring atmosphere, impressive connection speed, and plenty of seating. The entrance is free (bag search). Gets a bit busy about 30 min before the performance (currently Inter Alia with Rosamund Pike). The building is a notable example of post-WWII Brutality architecture. While its radical minimalism and anti-aesthetics is not for everyone, it was fitting well the post-WWII resources deprivation and priorities re-evaluation (i.e. the shift from pompous public projects to NHS and other projects that prioritize human lives). Creative night lighting, both inside and outside, adds an extra visual appeal while preserving the core concepts of the style. | Richard S(5): An absolute masterpiece of brutalist architecture, with an incredible use of space, well worth a visit, even if you don’t see a play. Lots of wonderful places to eat, drink and sit. The bookstore is fabulous.  Pictures don’t really capture it. | Andre Paie(5): Visiting the National Theatre in London was a truly enriching experience. From the moment I entered the building, I was struck by the architectural elegance and the welcoming, vibrant atmosphere. The space manages to feel both grand and intimate, with thoughtful design that invites you to slow down, observe, and soak in the cultural energy.  I attended a performance of [insert name of the play or production here], and it exceeded all my expectations. The direction was sharp, the pacing flawless, and the performances from the cast were deeply compelling. Every actor brought nuance and authenticity to their roles, and the emotional weight of the story was palpable throughout the auditorium.  What stood out most to me was the staging and set design—visually stunning, with clever transitions and lighting that enhanced the narrative without ever overshadowing it. The sound design and live music (if applicable) were also beautifully integrated, creating an immersive environment that kept the audience fully engaged.  The National Theatre staff were professional and courteous, helping to create a smooth and enjoyable experience from beginning to end. Even the intermission felt like part of the magic, with spaces to relax, grab a drink, and discuss what we’d seen so far.  In short, the National Theatre delivers not just a show, but an entire journey. It’s a place where powerful storytelling, artistic innovation, and human connection converge. I left the theatre inspired, moved, and already planning my next visit.
gmaps_editorial_summary: Home to prominent theatre company presenting new plays, classic drama and Shakespeare productions.

gmaps_reviews_top3: SH Naeem(5): I recently visited the SEA LIFE Aquarium in London and had a wonderful experience. The exhibits are well-designed, with a wide variety of marine life from sharks and rays to colorful tropical fish. The layout makes it easy to explore, and I especially enjoyed the underwater tunnel—it really felt like walking through the ocean!  The staff were friendly and knowledgeable, always ready to answer questions and share interesting facts. Everything was clean, well-organized, and family-friendly. It’s a perfect spot for both kids and adults to enjoy and learn about marine creatures.  I definitely recommend a visit if you’re in London and looking for a fun and educational experience. | Philip Uche-Obasi(5): Surprisingly magical — penguins, jellyfish, and sharks all in one place  I wasn’t sure what to expect, but SEA LIFE London Aquarium really impressed me. The layout flows nicely, and each section feels like stepping into a different world. The penguins were a highlight — playful and oddly calming to watch. The jellyfish room was stunning, like floating through a neon dream. And the shark tunnel? Genuinely thrilling. Seeing them glide overhead was surreal.  It’s not massive, but it’s packed with variety and atmosphere. Great for escaping the noise of the city for a couple of hours. Definitely worth a visit if you’re into marine life or just want something a bit different. | Sabbir ahmed(5): A great experience with amazing sea life, from sharks to penguins. The displays are beautiful and staff are friendly and knowledgeable. It’s fun and educational for all ages, but the ticket prices are quite high for the time spent inside. Still worth visiting at least once! 🐠🐧🌊

gmaps_reviews_top3: s tee(5): Once inside you come through double doors that open and can follow the footsteps on the floor which will guide you inside. Staff are friendly and welcoming. The space is clean, spacious and brightly lit. The colour chairs and tables add some character into the waiting space and the spot lights across the ceiling help build the environment. Such a great place that is not too far from the Oakwood station, it was actually a nice stroll maybe 10-12 minutes away on a straight road. Beside them is a small park with a playground and the cherry blossom trees which are in full bloom at the moment. They do have a cafe here so you can have a hot cuppa before or after the show. Everything is one level which means it’s buggy/stroller accessible. The team gave out stickers when we entered which nice.  The show is fantastic full of energy, live narration, vocals singing and dancing. My little one kept dancing and touching the puppets. All of the character were fun and colorful. The entire crew were so happy and energetic always making eye contact with everyone in the crowd and getting the kids to dance with all of their body. They even teach you sign languages which is great. As an adult I could see why this show was so popular and they always encouraged children to come to the stage and get involved. Lots of dancing positive vibes. I think this may have been the best children’s show I have been to so far and I would 100% go again. I also love how everyone is in Matt’s in the floor and were able to really see and hear everyone in the show.  You can even purchase merchandise and support by becoming a friend of Chickenshed. Overall the space is very organized, clean and fun I would recommend to friends and family and I would come back in future for other shows. Not to forget to mention the beautiful outdoor space that they have in the side which is open during the summer. The lights on top make it feel like a magical space for the children to enjoy.  We did join the first stay and play session they had one for £3 per child, it had colouring, drums, hula hoops, shapes to play with the light and shadow, foam squares and headband wear. The team are full of energy and enthusiasm. They really enjoy playing with children and encouraging them to join in. | Graham(5): Totally Tales was brilliant and thoroughly enjoyed by my grandchildren. So inclusive to all children each one made to feel special. Thank you. Marion Firth x | Tal Berman Howarth(5): I love Chickenshed! Best theatre and incredible ethos. They've been accepting everyone and adapting naturally for over 50 years to ensure anyone can participate and shine. Their December musical shows are as good or better than any I've seen in the West End. Full of heart! And bucketloads of talent 💗
gmaps_editorial_summary: Local theatre space in a former shed, offering drama and music performances and kids' shows.

A true humbling experience of the world of science  The exhibits are interactive, fun, and packed with fascinating information perfect for both kids and adults. I especially loved how hands on so many of the displays are; it makes learning feel exciting and engaging rather than just reading from a plaque.  The museum covers such a wide range of topics, from space and engineering to medicine and technology, so there’s truly something for everyone. The museum has so many fascinating exhibits, so plan to spend plenty of time if you want to see everything. It’s a great place to learn about science—whether it’s how the human body works or the history of the steam train. Kids can explore, play, and learn in a very hands-on way, which makes it even more engaging. Not to mention that all staff was cool and friendly.  There are dining areas and some lovely gift shops to enjoy as well. Tickets can be purchased online in advance, or directly at the entrance on the day of your visit. | Christine G(5): The Science Museum is such a fun and interesting place to visit! There’s so much to see and do for all ages—from space rockets and historic inventions to hands-on experiments and interactive zones for kids. We don’t have anything like this in Toronto, so it was a real treat to explore. It’s free to enter (some exhibits cost extra), and you can easily spend hours here. Definitely a must-visit if you’re in London!

gmaps_reviews_top3: Alan Gilbert(5): I wasn't sure what to expect before I went but I'm so glad I visited. My Nan attended this school from late 1889 to early 1892. It was amazing to stand in the very room where she would have been taught. The reconstructed classroom is excellent and the school group that were visiting while I was there seemed to enjoy being taught the "Victorian way" by an actress playing the part of a schoolmistress. The exhibits and information about the Ragged School Movement is very interesting and isn't too overwhelming in content. There is also a reconstruction of a typical house from the Victorian era. It's difficult to imagine how families lived in such conditions. I thoroughly recommend this museum. It's well worth the modest entry fee. | Sadek Miah(4): A hidden gem that deserves a lot more exposure as it highlights just how tragic life was for Victorian parents who couldn't afford education for their children, and how the concept of Ragged School sought to correct this gap for the thousands of impoverished kids living in London. There's an entire classroom that gives you an idea of how basic the day to day learning was and it's actually used even now to give kids today an idea of just how simple the classrooms back then really were. There's also a room above the classroom that paints a picture of what extreme poverty looked like in Victorian London and it makes for a sombre experience. Definitely a museum that's under the radar but one that you should check out to learn just how tragically difficult it was for Victorian children to have an education - a tool that by right should be universal for all, no matter who you are or where you're from. | Shanaz Rashid(5): Great experience my 6 year old who loved it. We attended the Victorian classroom which is every 1st Sunday of the month. We combined it with a walk down the canal and some playtime in Mile end park. You wouldn’t more than two hours there. There’s parking available on the weekends on the street.
gmaps_editorial_summary: Victorian school museum in converted warehouse with replica classroom and interactive activities.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Vahid Serpooshan(3): Copthall Leisure Centre has its strengths, but also some areas that really need improvement.  Starting with the gym – it’s a decent size and has a good range of equipment, but it’s consistently overcrowded, especially during peak hours. Unfortunately, poor ventilation makes the space feel stuffy and uncomfortable, especially during intense workouts. It definitely could use better airflow or air conditioning.  On a more positive note, the swimming facilities are excellent. The pools are well-maintained and the swimming classes are top-notch. The coaches are experienced and friendly, and there’s a wide variety of intensive classes that cater to all levels – this is easily the best part of the centre.  The café is okay – not spectacular, but it does the job. They offer a solid selection of protein shakes, which is great post-workout, though the rest of the menu could be a bit more exciting.  Overall, Copthall Leisure Centre has potential. If they improve the ventilation and manage gym crowding better, it could be a much more enjoyable place to train. | Han Chee Tan(1): One of the worst experiences I’ve had at a leisure centre in the UK.  Upon arrival, we had to queue for about 5 minutes at reception. The receptionist specifically told me to go to the competition pool after I showed him my mobile booking. When we got there, we were told to go to the community pool because there was a lesson taking place. At the community pool, we were redirected AGAIN —this time to the learner pool, because another lesson was running. That’s ridiculous—I paid for the competition pool but ended up in the learner pool!  After about 15 minutes, a staff member came over and told me we could now go to the competition pool as the lesson had overrun. What a JOKE! It honestly felt like we were being messed around the entire time.  By the time we finally got in, we only had about 20 minutes left before 11:50 a.m. On top of that, we were told we could only swim in a restricted area, since the rest of the pool wasn’t suitable for children who couldn’t manage the deep end. That left us confined to a tiny 6 x 12.5 metre SECTION!!!  My only advice: think twice before planning a visit here. Don’t get me wrong—the facilities themselves are fantastic—but the management is absolutely TERRIBLE!!! | Kim Charles(5): Today I had the pleasure of being assisted by a lovely and professional young lady called Lamia. I was amazed at the way she was able to help me without making me feel rushed and flustered. She was very respectful and help everyone in a polite manner. Coptall needs more of Lamia....
gmaps_editorial_summary: Home of Saracens rugby club and a leisure centre with a swimming pool, gym and driving range.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Local Guise(5): What a gem of a place for the local community!! Located in the most beautiful settings; adjacent to Montrose Park and opposite Silkstream Park, it just cannot be missed. I was welcomed by a Receptionist with the best custom. Then an employee had some informal chat whilst I was waiting. It was excellent to know that the young man had been promoted from being a service user to an employee, owing to the understanding eye of the support workers. The place is quieter during the day as most of the sessions are one-to-one but the children start pouring in good numbers after 4:00pm. Weekends are busy too as I have myself witnessed with my girl. There is a well defined area allocated to each activity available. There is wall/rock climbing, Roller skating, skate boarding, boxing and basketball ball court, Music room with a small attached recording room , Arts and Craft, Health and Well being room(rightly equipped) Enterprise room , a well equipped kitchen for cooking classes, a Conference suite  and a hall to hold different events. The hall that the reception desk opens into is speckled with different table games. The gym has got a host of equipment that can be utilised by adults and children(with age suitability) The outside space has got typical garden tables/chairs or even benches and a small wooden stage to hold small events. It must be a treat in the summers(You are allowed to use the space in winters too if you are adventurous) .The roof top pitch is icing on the cake nestled within netting all over to avoid any balls going astray. In many places the hard work of the children is also reflected. They have themselves decorated and painted the walls of different rooms to their own sensibility(aided and supervised by their tutors, instructors and coaches) There is a small cosy library caters to the book lovers. The children have the regular support of English and Maths Tutors too if required. In short, the place is remarkable. An epitome of excellence. You pay £5 for an Annual membership and 50p for the activities. It is a fairly new establishment.(started in 2019) but has seen the onslaught of the pandemic. However following the Government guidelines, they have operated when advised. I think the success of such places owes to the interest of the locals. There is SO MUCH MORE than you could see from outside or even hear. Better experience it first hand. I have had the opportunity of meeting the personnel and can assure you that they are sincere to the cause of promoting wellness , Health and Inclusion of the Community. They are open to all residents of Barnet and have currently members from Burnt Oak, Kingsbury, Finchley , Colindale, Mill Hill, Edgware and several others. Do join and commit yourselves to some productive use of your leisure time. | Muhammad(5): I put 5 stars because Fatima is so kind and really nice so is all the staff at unit as I love this place | SweetOne(5): Excellent facility for the local community. The children are always happy to return.

gmaps_reviews_top3: John Crawford(5): Although we've been many times to this beautiful park, today was the first time we hired recumbent bikes (banana bikes) and it was absolutely brilliant! My two daughters (9 and 6) took to them straightaway, although the younger one struggled initially with the steering.  They both said it was fantastic fun and we're already planning another visit of the next few weeks.  DEFINITELY recommend! | Guy Atherfold(5): Great setup and no fuss whatsoever. Fun for all the family or friends. Do make sure you check what is available as there is a number of different bikes to choose from.  The sit down bikes we had are hard work, but fun and the trike for my daughter was great for her. You get 1 hour which will get you a few laps round the park.  Get there early, the afternoons can get busy. | Claer Barrett FT(5): An easy walk from Battersea Power Station and a great experience to do with children. The roads in the park are wide and flat, and three different sizes of recumbent bikes can be hired depending on age of your kids (plus they also have normal bikes, trikes and a bike with a “bucket seat” on the front into which smaller children can be strapped in). The cost is £17 per hour (per bike!) but on the day we visited they were not very busy so we were given an extra 20 mins for free. The staff are super helpful and can adjust bikes in the blink of an eye and answer all of your questions, we even changed one of ours after 10 mins for a bigger size and nothing was too much of a problem. They will look after bags too if needed. The rest of Battersea Park is also amazing; there is an excellent (free!) playground with huge slides, a zip wire and mini outdoor trampolines at the other end of the park next to the (paid for) Go Ape treetop facility. Plenty of toilets and tea / coffee / ice cream places in park too and lots of wide open green spaces for ball games too, so you can easily make a day out of it.

name:

description_full:
A well established and vibrant multi-sports club based at Willesden Sports Centre. Open to all disabled people aged 11 + and any siblings too.
A three hour sports session covering a host of sports that can be adapted to all abilities. Popular sports include, basketball, cricket and boccia. The club plays termly cricket matches vs Cricket for Change clubs.
There is also a convenient seating area for parents and carers to watch with a gym and cafe also available at the centre.
Club Details

content:
Club Brent
A well established and vibrant multi-sports club based at Willesden Sports Centre. Open to all disabled people aged 11 + and any siblings too.

A three hour sports session covering a host of sports that can be adapted to all abilities. Popular sports include, basketball, cricket and boccia. The club plays termly cricket matches vs Cricket for Change clubs.

There is also a convenient seating area for parents and carers to watch with a gym and cafe also available at the centre.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Lisa Cao(1): Outrageous experience – completely unacceptable behavior, possibly racist  We are absolutely furious with the way we were treated at Talacre Community Sports Centre. We had a confirmed badminton court booking through the Better UK App — with a confirmation email to prove it. But when we arrived, we were told the court had been “booked by someone else” due to a system error, and we were forcibly kicked out by an incredibly rude and aggressive manager.  Instead of helping resolve the situation, the manager dismissed our confirmation, refused to listen, talked over us the entire time, and repeatedly told us to leave. His tone and behavior were not only deeply unprofessional, but downright hostile. It was clear he had no intention of treating us with fairness or respect.  We strongly suspect that racial discrimination played a role in how we were treated. We were not given the courtesy of a conversation, just immediately blamed and removed — despite having proof of our booking.  Five of us travelled significant distances to be there — some drove over an hour, others took the Underground, and I personally spent money on two Uber trips. We wasted time, money, and energy, only to be humiliated by a manager who acted like we were the problem when the fault was clearly with Better’s broken system and his abusive attitude. | Laura Hadrik(5): It's quite a nice place to go, the soft play area at first looks small, but has 3 levels and it actually varies and has lots of different fun climbing/ sliding options. The only downside is if you miss a session, you get charged, but if your instructor doesn’t show up to a session, you receive no reimbursement for this. They also tell you if a session is cancelled only when you arrive/are waiting for your class to start. Ideally they should let you know in advance, just like we have to do if we can’t make it to a class. A lot of wasted time | Luiz Pimenta(3): I’ve been attending this gym for a while, and while it does have some positives, there are several major issues that need to be addressed.  First, the organization of the free weights is a disaster. There’s no system in place, and people don’t bother to return weights to their designated spots. While the staff makes an effort to tidy up, they simply can’t keep up with the constant mess left behind by members.  The gym’s layout is another major downside. One section is always overcrowded, while other areas remain practically empty. This poor design isn’t just inconvenient; it’s also unsafe. Equipment is placed too close together, creating a risk of injury. Whoever planned the setup clearly didn’t consider functionality or safety.  On the positive side, the natural light is fantastic. The large windows fill the space with daylight, creating a more pleasant environment. Additionally, the gym has plenty of new equipment, which is a definite plus. The swimming facilities are also a nice bonus for those who enjoy using them.  However, cleanliness is a significant issue, particularly in the men’s toilets. They are consistently dirty, and the smell is unbearable. For a facility charging full price, this level of hygiene is unacceptable.  Overall, this gym might be worth it if you qualify for a discount (e.g., for unemployment or disability). But at full price, there are better and more affordable options nearby.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Emma T(5): ⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️ | Riverside Leisure Centre: More Than Just an Ice Rink (But Good Luck With the Vending Machines)  Ah, Riverside Leisure Centre - where I spend so much time, I may as well set up a bed in the corner. My daughter plays ice hockey here, and compared to some of the horrifying ice rinks we travel to for matches (see my other scathing reviews for tales of tragic toilets), this place is actually really good.  The facilities are great - at least, I assume they are, since most of them are closed by the time training kicks off at 9:45 PM. Want a post-practice snack? Hope you like the thrill of gambling with a vending machine that’s either empty, broken, or requires a PhD to operate. I often find myself coaching a group of confused teenagers from the Friday night disco skate (think nightclub on ice - smoke machines, chaos, and teens with questionable balance) on how to work the thing. If only they’d put me on payroll.  The ice? Solid. Seating? Decent. The PA system? A complete mystery. Match announcements sound like they’re being transmitted from a WW2 bunker - muffled, garbled, and completely unintelligible. But hey, it adds to the drama.  Oh, and parking? I haven’t paid in three years, so I’m assuming it’s free. Or I’m on the run from some serious fines. Either way, bonus!  Would I recommend? Definitely. Just bring snacks and don’t rely on the PA system to know what’s going on. | Adam Wells (Croblis)(1): Great... When you can get in. description_full:
WeSwim Barbican
If you have any general questions about swimming with WeSwim you can view them here.
Where?
Golden Lane Leisure Centre is based at Fann St, Fane Lane, Golden Lane Estate, London EC1Y 0SH, 0.2 miles from Barbican Station and is a multi-sport community sports centre.
There are steps or a pedestrian ramp to access to the leisure centre, with level access once inside. Access to the pool is via a ladder or chair hoist. Lockers require a padlock.
We have dedicated use of the 20m pool, where you will be supported by our team of friendly, enthusiastic and trained volunteers, providing a safe, private environment to swim in.
When?
The weekly session will be held on Wednesdays from 7pm to 8pm. After your swim there will be an opportunity to chat to other swimmers and volunteers over a well earned refreshments in the swimming pool reception.
Who?
The sessions and community at WeSwim Barbican will be led by a fabulous committee, who will also act as our safeguarding officers and will manage a team of volunteers to support our swimmers.
Cost?
We ask swimmers for a £2.00 contribution per session to help support the on-going operation of the club. If you have worries about being able to pay this on a regular basis please talk to tash@weswim.club.
https://weswim.club/barbican/

gmaps_reviews_top3: Byron Taylor(1): Quite simply the worst run swimming pool I have ever used. Repeatedly cancelling morning sessions. Repeatedly promising refunds that never arrive. It’s a beautiful historic pool being run very very badly. Oh yeah, and their HQ doesn’t respond to complaints.  I am sad to say I’m defecting to the nearby Ironmonger Public Baths. | Zowie Georgia(5): Really hope this place gets the local support they need to keep it going and to make general improvements - e.g showers. Also - as we’re approaching summer time would be great to extend the swimming hours and have some continuity with this schedule please instead of such sporadic timings. gmaps_reviews_top3: Izzy M(5): I'm a new-ish gym goer and I go here for the gym, treadmill terrace, sometimes the sauna and have used the squash courts a couple of times. Such a great facility for the community. It feels like they care about making it a good space for everyone (they've made a bunch of improvements like updating the layout of the gym, new equipment, bigger soft play area). It feels pretty friendly and welcoming to me. Really rate this leisure centre | Marwa(2): Recently booked an annual membership here and I'm saddened to report how disappointed I am.  I came here on Tuesday for the morning Swim for Fitness booking and the evening Swim for Women booking. In the morning, the main pool had a nice temperature and it was deep. Yet as i was doing my laps, i could see shocking objects floating past me: hair, plasters, chewing gum. The same thing applies to the shallow pool. It was absolutely disgusting seeing the amount of hair in the pool and the unknown objects floating by. My face felt waxy and dirty after my session, something that has never happened to me at other Better centres.  The changing rooms are cramped and dirty. The shower space is extremely small and barely functioning. I felt so claustrophobic showering in the cubicle, with its low water pressure but at least the water was warm.  I'm appaled and embarrased to say that I've caught ringworm which has affected my quality of life and the people around me.  Clissold is a beautiful and nostalgic place but there needs to be immediate changes to ensure every user can leave in a healthy and satisfied state. I have read your replies that insinuate there will hopefully be new changes with the changing rooms. Please, listen to your customers and take our personal experiences into consideration | yisroel levin(5): Clissold Leisure Centre in Stoke Newington offers a modern gym, spacious sports hall, and swimming pools for all ages. With diverse fitness classes, children’s activities, and spa facilities, it caters to a wide range of visitors. Grossly overpriced. I have been a member for 6 years. The reversion to Southwark council management filled members with optimism that things would improve. It's the opposite. Things are worse than ever. And prices have doubled, with zero relief nor recognition for members who helped with maintenance costs during covid shutdowns in exchange for long term privileges; nor avenues for low income earners to seek fair adjustments. gmaps_reviews_top3: Leanne McCarthy(5): For the MayWhit cafe! Had a really tasty scrambled eggs and Avocado on toast, it had a little salad and pomegranate and sunflower seeds, I asked for a wet scramble and it was perfect! Tables clean, calm environment! Lovely coffee! Great to access a cafe in a leisure center that isn’t just serving chips! 👍🏽 (Unfortunately I’d eaten it all before I thought to take a pic) | Anushka Camm(3): This is a review directed towards the MayWhit  cafe. The Better Health gym is excellent. Usually, our experience is 5 stars, but todays its three  Me and my partner (who have invisible disabilities) arrived a little too early to go swimming, so my partner went to go sit in the cafe while i went to the loo. We often sit in the cafe and wait to go in, and we haven't experienced this before.  While i was in the loo, my partner was targeted by the dark haired lady (after the blonde lady was whispering to to her while looking at my partner), demanding him to order something, while holding a card machine. He explained he was waiting for me to come back and to go into the pool, but she asked him to leave if he wasnt ordering anything! There were multiple people (mostly women, and children, i may add) who were waiting like he was and they were not made to order anything. There was even people eating lunch they had brought with them.  When my partner told me what happened, i decided to go back into the cafe by myself and sit quietly - like my partner. Yhe blonde haired lady even came over to clean the table mext to me, so my presence was known. I was not demanded to order anything, nor leave. This proved that - for whatever reason - they did not want a lone, male patron in their cafe. Even if that was not what they intended, its definitely how it came across to us.  I saw another review similar to this one, and its shocking that this isn't the first time this has happened. | Ion Ceban(1): My kid was waiting for this party 1 week! On the paper no information about the adult need to assist the child! I was thinking teachers will make party, because on the paper it's school swimming party!(swimming school membership) Soo they don't let me assist my kid ( because I don't have swimming clothes)and my kid can't go on swimming pool! Just left crying...Thank you ELTHAM Center!!!

One ended up having a massive deformity and the other had no eyes. We are giving them a good life but wouldn’t recommend sourcing an axolotl from this shop. | Andrew John(1): Quite possibly the worst gym I've been to.  1. Tiny, unattractive and claustrophobic gym with Technogym machines (considered some of the worst in the business, all awkward designs/poor pumps), a small dumbbell rack area with only 3 benches and 2 sets of cables that are either broken or hogged. 1 bench press and 2 squat racks. Cardio wise in the gym, 2-3 rowing machines, 4 Spinning bikes and about 9 treadmills, the latter two which are pretty much all occupied at peak hours.  2. Gym etiquette from members is appalling. After 1600 hours, you have a bunch of consistent 3-5 young teenagers that hog machines and benches (only 3) and congregate the small spaces. Sadly, some of these teenagers have ZERO etiquette. Expect behaviour like curling in front of the dumbbell racks, resting during curls on the 3 benches and bullying and hogging of equipment.  3. Tragic carpark system with only a one-way enter system, complete with only about 30 bays in total. I've also seen several Police cars called to the gym a few times in the last 2 years, god knows why and honestly not a good look to the reputation of the gym.  4. Only positives for me in the gym are the monkey bars (pretty standard), the quality of the Technogym Dumbbells and the generosity of Dumbbell rack in how high the weights go up.  All of this for £47 a month is a daylight robbery. The only reason I guess you pay that is because there is no other competition in Epping. If a Puregym or The Gym opened up in this town, I imagine Epping Sports Centre would lose 50% of their members.

content:
The Epping Forest Inclusion Project provides sports, leisure and physical activity opportunities for children and young people with SEND. During term time the project puts on a number of out of school sessions ranging from Trampolining to Horse Riding. During the schools holidays various sessions and activities are held, which where possible include siblings and parents. Activities on our holiday programme include Own a Pony, Relaxed Performances, Forest Fun days and Multi Sports Camps.

Tel: 01992 564561

Email: csreception@eppingforestdc.gov.uk

name: Cycling -Finsbury Park
description_full:
http://www.pedalpowercc.org/
Pedal Power is the cycling club for teenagers and adults with learning disabilities. We meet on the Finsbury Park basketball courts on Tuesdays 11:00am-2:00pm, and fortnightly on Saturdays between 12:00 and 4:00.
Absolutely FREE.
Please wear suitable footwear.
Helmets are necessary and provided for those who don’t have their own. Carers and support workers are encouraged to join in. Our trainers are qualified National Standards cycle trainers.
Sessions feature:
• Cycling for complete beginners on 2 wheels or trikes
• Games to improve cycling skills
• An opportunity to try for the Special Olympics
• Specialised bikes for a whole range of disability
• Off road location.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Hayley J Bacon(4): VISIT DATE: SAT 13/09/2025 VISIT TIME: 12:00 - 13:00  Situated inside Mayesbrook Park, this centre comprises of a large hall which is divided in half, with one section set up as a trampoline jump park and the other as a regular sports hall, which can be used for a battery of activities.  The centre is accessible, with step-free access, lifts and accessible toilets, but the trampoline area is not.  There is a café on the first floor which sells a range of hot and cold drinks, snacks and sandwiches, all at the prices you’d expect for this sort of place. gmaps_reviews_top3: Alex Sim(5): Friendly staff, reasonable prices, quite clean and lots of pools and space. However if the pool is busy there is sometimes a poolside wait to get into the pool. Overall a pretty good option for outing for the family. | Saran Kugathas(2): The guy in the picture just don’t know how to deal with customers. He made us wait for almost 1 hour just for a bloody voucher. He didn’t even apologise. One of the pool was closed and they didn’t tell us when they took our money! I mean what kind of service is this? Awful! | Guy Bates(5): Good 50m pool. I swim virtually every weekday morning for over 2 kilometres and generally at least one day at weekends and am happy with it. Swimming in a 50m pool is much more pleasant than a 25m one. It's more reliable now then it was 20 years ago in terms of both machinery  and staff. Access is punctual and the staff are friendly and efficient.  Grass and fields nearby, pleasant scenery. Not surrounded by housing and appears to be in a green corridor! The surrounds would be a good deal less pleasant after the proposed demolition and rebuild amidst a large housing development on metropolitan open land and a flood plain.  Residential and office buildings of the same age have a vastly longer expected life than the redevelopment project estimates for the current building. I have been told that a quarter of the roof was recently refurbished with a 25 year guarantee for 27 thousand pounds which is thought provoking when set against project estimates of 1 million for 100% roof repair and 5 million for replacement.
gmaps_editorial_summary: Rare 50m pool with a smaller leisure pool, sauna, steam room and exercise studios with classes.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Frank Zagarino(1): We brought our son to Access to Sport half term club in Millfields park. He has long hair. And on the first day, he was called gay by other slightly older children (aged 10-12) as they played football because of his long hair. He was not mistaken for a girl (I accept that to happen sometimes by mistake - easily corrected), but he was intentionally profiled as gay due to his hair. For reference - half of the boys in his class have long hair. I see this as homophobic bullying that is not acceptable. While the staff are not responsible for what children say, the staff have clearly not set any ground rules at the beginning of the day / half term club. And although they said, once the issue was raised, that they will address this with the bullies, the staff made no efforts the next day to include our son in the club activities in the morning when he was quite reluctant to rejoin. So a double fail. | Chris Eye(5): My daughter really enjoyed the holiday camp this Easter. The team that held the sports club at elthrone Park were amazing. So caring, professional and fun. I would totally recommend them to everyone and will definitely be signing my daughter up again. Thanks for amazing half term for my daughter. | Neil Kelleher(5): Amazing program my 5 year old absolutely loved it. So much better than time on iPads and made firm friends within the first day of the summer program.  Thank you team !

name: Cycling -Bushy Park Hampton Crt
description_full:
Saturday 11.00am to 1.00pm
TW12 2EJ, United Kingdom
http://www.bikeworks.org.uk/
Companion Cycling is a scheme which enables people who are unable to ride “solo” cycles for whatever reason, to enjoy the pleasure of cycling with a companion in the delightful and traffic free surroundings of Bushy Park, adjacent to Hampton Court in South West London.Anyone, whatever their ability or mobility, can become a cyclist using one of our special cyclesand with the aid of a “pilot” who might be a carer, a friend, a family member or one of our volunteers.Our wide variety of special cycles are carefully selected to enable people to ride whatever their ability. These range from side-by-side pedal cycles suited to those with good mobility but limited co-ordination, through to a wheelchair ‘rickshaw’ suited to riders with severe mobility problems. In addition we have a range of tricycles with added safety features, a tandem and various conventional bicycles for accompanying riders. Our volunteers will help you to choose the cycle which is most suited to your needs, and will familiarise new riders and pilots with the cycles and the routes around the park.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Kap Pin(2): The swimming pool itself was very nice and enjoyable to use.  Unfortunately, the experience was let down by other aspects. The receptionist came across as unfriendly, and the interaction felt very robotic rather than welcoming.  The showers and changing rooms were not up to standard, with chewing gum collecting in the drains and an overall impression that they had not been cleaned for some time.  Having moved out of London several years ago, I’ve noticed that service and standards tend to be higher in other English counties. Perhaps my expectations are too high, but I do feel facilities should be maintained and presented to a better standard. | Monica H(4): I visited the soft play with my 2yo and autistic 5yo.  They both enjoyed themselves very much and had plenty to do.  The soft play was clean, safe and very quiet on a weekday morning.  It only has one entrance/ exit, making it ideal for managing multiple young children and children with SEND who elope.  There was also a staff member in the soft play who was quietly observing and supporting when needed (ie: to wipe up water spills).  The only thing that stopped this being a 5 star review was the reception experience.  There was a very long queue for the leisure centre and only one staff member working! It would be good if we could just scan the online booking and be let in automatically or if there were 2 reception staff— as it appears to be a busy leisure centre with many activities. | Mohammad Hamid Ebadi(1): Review: 0/5 – Extremely Disappointing Experience  I had a very unpleasant experience at the Harrow Luicer Center. name:

Hendon
Sarrie's Skills Club
Saracens Rugby Club
description_full:
This programme is the pioneer in itself as it is the first provision specifically for autism to be run by a professional sports teams in the UK. We use multi-sport sessions with each member having a tailored training programme in order to suit their specific needs.
The aim of the sessions are to improve the physical capability of each individual as well as using it as a vehicle to improve basic life skills such as social interaction, sleep and their behaviour. In order to achieve these aims each member will have at least 1 on 1 support which consists of both staff and volunteers who are all devoted and passionate in aiding towards the cause.
Whilst our members are down stairs blasting through their activities, we provide a respite area to parents and carers which takes place in our hospitality lounge, where they are able take a well-earned rest or share some of their stories with others.
If you would to know more information about this project, please contact: Benjamin Lawrence –DisAbility Sport Development Officer - Tel: 0203 675 7243 - Email: benjaminlawrence@saracens.net
http://www.saracens.com/foundation/inclusion/sarries-skill-club

Horizon Sports Club
description_full:
Horizon welcomes all children with a disability from the age of 4 years within South Buckinghamshire.
Horizon operates two sections within the club. Our junior section accommodates 4–11 year olds and provides one-one support from volunteer coaching assistants. Our youth section also has volunteers supporting them, if that is deemed appropriate.
Wycombe Leisure Centre
Marlow Hill, High Wycombe,
Buckinghamshire HP11 1TJ
http://www.horizonsportsclub.org.uk
Tel: 01494 349001
email: info@horizonsportsclub.org.uk
Activities
-Horizon Minis
Tuesday 4.00pm
This class formerly is aimed at members aged between 4 and 8 years old. It will give our members the opportunity to experience, learn and develop a wide range of skills and take part in a number of games with a specific focus on exercises to support their individual needs.
-Junior Swimming
Wednesday 6.30pm and 7.20pm
Horizon junior swimming classes present our members with the valuable opportunity to become confident and most importantly safe in the water. Our swimming teachers will work each week to develop the knowledge and skills required to achieve the ultimate outcome of independent swimming. His class is for age 7-13 years old.
-Junior Ball Skills
Wednesday 5.15pm and Thursday 6.00pm
This class is designed to allow our members the opportunity to further develop all skills associated with ball-based games. Each week will have a specific sports focus ranging from football to cricket and everything in between. It will not just be the skills of the game but also the rules that will be taught. Each session will conclude with small-sides games to encourage team work and introduce members to more competitive situations. This class is aimed at ages 7-13.

This can sometimes be made more frustrating by the fact that the lessons are not always taking place in all of the designated lanes at the time. | Blue Mahoe(1): Arrived early for the first swim with my young primary school aged daughter, who had a swimming competition later in the day and felt nervous about her backstroke, poor thing, but we've all been there with nerves.  We paid a stonking £9 for her to swim (I wasn't going in). However, the pool was packed. There were three people in each lane, every lane. 8.10 in the morning! I'd say the vast majority were older than 40 or 50, though it can be hard to tell. Of course there were lanes of various speed, but my daughter was going to be swimming the 25 metres in less than 20 seconds, she would quite clearly have had to slow down (therefore not preparing for the competition) or just steaming through and colliding with the person in front. That wouldn't work for anyone. She hadn't even gone in, so I politely asked for a refund for her. The swim manager said no as she could join a lane as the fourth person. Yes, of course she could in theory, but in practice it wouldn't work. The manager still insisted no, it wasn't possible. He went to walk off, as he did I asked him his name (I won't write it here) and asked whether anyone had ever done him a favour, you know let him off. He looked at me sheepishly and said yes, they had, but he wasn't going to do us a favour in this instance because he'd have to explain the refund to his boss and he didn't want to go through that.  Honestly,  the pool was jam packed, they didn't look like they needed our £9. I thanked him for his time and left with my daughter to Northolt pool, just a ten minute drive away. £2.60 for her swim and a lane to herself! And she achieved the sub-20 seconds in the race a few hours ago, no thanks to Highgrove Pool.  Honestly guys, this was so mean. A child wanting to practice swimming for a competition, making no fuss, just asking for her £9 refund, and you couldn't do it?!  The lady at reception was lovely, and she had inspired me to join the pool at £40 for adult membership and £23 each for my 3 children as the pool is so local and convenient to us. Doing the maths that would have been £1308 per year. Lost over 9 quid!  This pool is not for me, but if you want a busy swim, with lots of feet in your face and staff who shrug their shoulders at you as they walk away, this is right up your street. | Bab Mami(1): I am a disabled person and had been going to this gym for a while. When I signed up, they knew about my disability and gave me a small discount. Nobody told me I had to book every time.  A few days ago, I was told I could not use the gym because I didn’t book — even though no one explained this before. My wife called and the staff admitted it was a mistake, and that I should always be allowed to come in.  But then, I was sitting in the steam room, and a lady came and told me I had to leave — in front of other people. I felt very embarrassed and disrespected. I didn’t do anything wrong. Because my English is not good, I felt helpless.  This experience made me cancel my membership. I hope they treat disabled people better in the future.

While the staff are not responsible for what children say, the staff have clearly not set any ground rules at the beginning of the day / half term club. And although they said, once the issue was raised, that they will address this with the bullies, the staff made no efforts the next day to include our son in the club activities in the morning when he was quite reluctant to rejoin. So a double fail. | Chris Eye(5): My daughter really enjoyed the holiday camp this Easter. The team that held the sports club at elthrone Park were amazing. So caring, professional and fun. I would totally recommend them to everyone and will definitely be signing my daughter up again. Thanks for amazing half term for my daughter. | Neil Kelleher(5): Amazing program my 5 year old absolutely loved it. So much better than time on iPads and made firm friends within the first day of the summer program.  Thank you team !

gmaps_reviews_top3: Abhijeet Roy(3): Nice place for kids to enjoy and play. Also, it is a very good arrangement to organise birthday parties. The only drawback is that it has a very small parking. I went there last Sunday and had to wait for a very long time to find a parking spot. The staff were very helpful and treated us very well. Overall, it was a nice place. | Matin Ahmadi(3): We recently hosted attended a birthday party at Oxygen Jumping in Acton, and while there were some positive aspects, the overall experience left much to be desired.  Parking and Accessibility: One of the most significant drawbacks was the parking situation. The lot is quite small, making it extremely challenging to find a spot, even if you’re willing to walk 15 minutes. The location feels isolated, which adds to the inconvenience.  Entry and Staff: Upon arrival, we encountered a lengthy waiting time to get inside. Once we finally made it in, we noticed that the staff seemed overwhelmed and not very attentive. It felt like they were stretched too thin to provide the level of service we expected.  Facilities: On a positive note, the facilities themselves are well-maintained and nice. However, they were incredibly overcrowded during our visit, which detracted from the enjoyment. It was difficult to fully appreciate the amenities when so many people were packed into the space.  Overall Experience: Given the price we paid, the experience was quite average. The combination of parking issues, long wait times, and overcrowding made it hard to recommend Oxygen Jumping for events, especially if you’re looking for a smooth and enjoyable experience.  In summary, while Oxygen Jumping has potential with its facilities, improvements in accessibility, staffing, and crowd management are needed to justify the costs and enhance visitor experience. | iva tania(5): Come here for the first time and really want to come back next time. Clean and well maintained playing areas with many helpful staffs to secure the children through their play time. The cafes and toilets are nice. A little bit complex access and parking for car doesn’t reduce kid’s impression for this fun place.

gmaps_reviews_top3: emrah hun(1): I am very disappointed with the service at Cascades Leisure Centre. I spent the entire day trying to call the centre, yet not a single call was answered. This is completely unacceptable for a public-facing business that parents and members rely on.  When I came in to drop my daughter for her swimming lesson, I asked Becky why no one answers the phone. Her response was, “If I’m not busy, I answer. I was busy.” This was shocking to hear — it shows a lack of professionalism and disregard for customers. Answering calls is not optional; it is a basic responsibility of any leisure centre.  The fact that such an important line of communication is being ignored raises serious concerns about how the centre is being managed. I explained that I could not access the information online, she simply repeated the same phrase instead of providing any real support.  When I asked for leaflets, she told me there were none available — only for me to later notice a leaflet about swimming lessons on display. gmaps_reviews_top3: An Q(1): Disappointing Experience at Newham Leisure Centre Football Pitch  We play football at Newham Leisure Centre every Wednesday and have been doing so regularly. Unfortunately, the experience has been consistently frustrating. The pitch is in poor condition – it’s mostly dirt, and the net is either missing or completely ripped. Despite this, we continue to book it (paying £77) simply because of its convenient location.  We normally book the pitch from 10am to 11am, but unfortunately, today we selected the wrong time and booked it for 11am to 12pm by mistake. Since all the players had arrived early and the pitch is always empty except for us, we politely requested for it to be opened at 10am. Ed, the staff member on duty, eventually opened the pitch at 10:50am, and then kicked us off at 11:30am – insisting that our time had started when he unlocked it. That left us with only 30 minutes of playtime, despite having paid for a full hour.  When I went to reception to speak to a manager (as Ed advised), it turned out he was the manager – making it very uncomfortable to raise our concerns. I explained that this isn’t a one-off issue: the pitch is rarely open on time, and we regularly have to ask staff to unlock it, which cuts into our paid time. Unfortunately, Ed was extremely unprofessional and dismissive during the conversation.  We are very disappointed with the way the facility is managed and the lack of customer care, especially for regulars. Hopefully, the centre takes feedback seriously and makes the necessary improvements.  We will never come back as a result you lost a regular customer and money as we see Wednesday no one booked pitch except Us. | Ismaeel Khan(5): Absolutely fantastic facility, love playing badminton here and hope to continue to do so for many years to come. | sarbjit “Terry” singh(2): Personal trainers have got a bad attitude. I was willing to hire a person's trainer. I will spend my money somewhere else.

name: Sport -Old Street Swimming Pool
description_full:
Our session at Old Street in Islington, London, is the first WeSwim club. It has excellent facilities and the use of two pools to meet people’s differing needs.
If you have any general questions about swimming with WeSwim you can view them here and specific questions related to WeSwim Old Street here.
Where?
Ironmonger Row Baths, at 1 Norman St EC1V 3AA has fantastic disabled access to the building, changing rooms and the swimming pool so whatever your disability, confidence or swimming level we would love to support you in exploring the mental, physical and social benefits of swimming and being in the water.
We have dedicated use of the 15m teaching pool & 2 lanes of the main pool at Ironmonger Row Baths, where you will be supported by our team of friendly, enthusiastic and trained volunteers, providing a safe, private environment to swim in.
When?
The weekly session are currently held on Tuesdays between 7.15 – 8.30pm. After your swim there will be an opportunity to chat to other swimmers and volunteers over a well earned refreshments in the swimming pool reception.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Snejana F.(3): We visited Oxygen Romford as a family of five — two adults and three children aged 17, 15, and 7 — hoping to spend some fun, active time together. Our goal was to enjoy it as a full-family activity, but the reality turned out to be different. Unfortunately, it quickly became clear that the park is not designed for older kids or adults. The teens were bored almost immediately — there was nothing engaging for their age group — and as adults, we didn’t feel comfortable jumping in the same space as small children. It felt unsafe and awkward, with a real risk of accidentally bumping into or injuring a younger child. In the end, four out of five tickets were basically wasted. That said, the experience was great for our 7-year-old — he had a fantastic time! The layout and activities are clearly aimed at kids under 10, and for that age group, it’s very enjoyable. Also, a big plus is the café area for parents. It offers a clear view of the entire trampoline zone, which means you can sit down, relax, and still keep an eye on your child at all times. That visibility is a huge advantage for parents.  Our suggestion: The website should clearly state the recommended maximum age for participants. It would help families avoid disappointment and ensure a safer environment for the younger children.  We might return — but only with our youngest next time. | Emily Napper(5): Staff are lovely. Took my nephew here for the first time. He loved it. Reception team were friendly and helpful. Play area was massive. Seating area was clean and thr coffee was good. | Edit Simon(5): I just had a great time at this venue! The team were absolutely brilliant – really welcoming and friendly throughout my visit, which made all the difference. Everything was spotless and the value for money was spot on. The only hiccup was the temperature; it was a bit too warm for comfort. Overall, I can't wait to go back!

Inclusive sessions include disabled participants, ensuring that additional help and support is provided when and if required. Dedicated sessions follow the inclusive gym class but allowing for 1:1 coach support work where it is required. Use of special adapted equipment as well as mainstream gymnastics equipment is used to enable all ability levels to participate and access gymnastics. Falcon Spartak allow carers and parents to assist in the gym. They can leave as and when they are happy to. Gymnasts can also be given a buddy to work with. All new gymnasts are allowed 3 taster sessions before the final decision is taken to join the club. this allows the gymnast and parent/carers time to decide if they like the club. Disabled Gymnasts have the opportunity to compete in regional and club competitions, displays, GymFusion, Disability National events in Rhythmic, Artistic and Trampolining. All coaches in the club have taken the disability add-on module (3 club coaches, 1 assistant coach, 1 trampoline coach and 1 senior club coach in Rhythmic) Club competitions take place before Christmas. The club also celebrates its 40th birthday in 2013 so there will be events to celebrate this. All events held at the club are fully inclusive and gymnasts from dedicated sessions are also encouraged to attend.

gmaps_reviews_top3: elena caviola(2): I truly appreciate the benefit of having access to the gym and swimming pool for free on weekends for Southwark council residents. However, the facility requires significant improvements in cleaning services and maintenance to ensure better conditions for users. The swimming pool shows signs of extensive mould and appears very old. The shower rooms are problematic, with poor water pressure and showers that last only 4-5 seconds. Additionally, one shower is excessively hot while the other is too cold, with no option to regulate the temperature. The facility has two hairdryers, but one is broken and temporarily fixed with tape. The gym is dusty, and the air conditioning frequently malfunctions. There is a noticeable lack of cleaning staff present during the day to maintain cleanliness in both the swimming pool and gym areas. Moreover, some lockers are broken, which is inconvenient for users. One positive aspect is the sauna, which is well-maintained and clean. | federica furlotti(1): Filthiest gym ever. I have been a paying member for two years when I lived in Peckham and used East Dulwich and Peckham leisure centres which were clean. Seven islands is shocking, health and safety does not exists there and the risk of getting infected with something is so high I am not sure why non shut it down. Anti bacterial spray, soap and other basic item to clean equipments are always missing, the people working at the gym who should check those things are replaced do nothing. It’s ridiculous. | AJP(1): Use to come here as a kid and needed a redurb then. Took my kids and was genuinely shocked that they are allowed to be open! Green everywhere, bodyfat and grit maybe even sand in the actual pool. I use to be a life guard, i know its incredibly boring, but lord almighty they all seem like they were on death row.  Couldn't possibly recommend. Some pictures below but they do not do the mess justice.

The toilets are often dirty, unflushed, with bins overflowing and unopenable and trash on the floor. gmaps_reviews_top3: Hannah(5): Absolutely love this model village. We went here on the drive home to pop in as we went when we were kids (now 30) and we enjoyed it just as much. It also brought back alot of memories for my parents and my dad spent the most time walking around and taking photos out of all of us (big kid at heart). We even took a ride on the little train for 5 minutes. Definitely a place for all ages and a fun day out. It also has a cafe and a little exhibition room which was great. | Dorota Trapl(5): The best place for little train-obsessed five year olds. We have visited the site last year and this year, our little one was constantly talking about it and we had to return. We love the place, it brings so much tranquility and soothe as it moves you in time and place of calm, order and simpler life. The scenery and each detail make you appreciate the way of life we have lost, seems so precious. Pay attention to details - like name on the shops and venues, make you giggle, the train stations and trains running everywhere (the bridge by the castle!). And the horticultural care - what a masterpiece of mini elms, pines, little rivers, cascades. There is a short train ride for families and you can have some fun with operating a boat on a pond. Plenty of koi carps, would be great if we could feed them, too! (Please sell the fish food with tickets!) We discovered new room where old type writer is, what a great fun to do some scenarios and play with props. Playground is also awesome for kids, good food selection in cafe (although I would like to see more healthier options) and plenty of seating area (you can bring your own food). Thank you for being there :) | Danutz(5): The most iconic model village visited so far. The miniature railway is very well presented and maintained.  With all the trains going up and down all around the miniature village. The manufacturers of the houses are made very nice with all the details even inside. Well done 👏
gmaps_editorial_summary: Original miniature village opened in 1929, with detailed 1930s buildings and an extensive railway.

full_address: Ebony Horse Club specialises in young people with emotional and behavioral difficulties, and welcomes those with special educational needs and some medical conditions. We are not a Riding for the Disabled Centre, but we do have a specialist instructor who is RDA-qualified. We do not have experience of all conditions and therefore assess our ability to accommodate each young person individually. If we are unable to accommodate a young person, we will recommend another centre with suitable expertise and facilities. The vast majority of those with special needs or disabilities are fully integrated within our regular classes and we currently have many riders on the autistic spectrum registered with us.

name: Nightlife East London Bubble Club -Access to Nightlife for everyone
description_full:
The original Bubble Club project grew from out of a series of meetings held in 2005 as part of an think tank called "Have Your Say" where people with learning disabilities were given the chance to speak out about the injustices they were experiencing in their local communities. Some of the major concerns being raised were widespread experiences of social isolation and loneliness. People reported feeling unsafe to go out in the evenings and lacked any opportunity to socialise in the evening.
A number of these people went on to pioneer the possibilities of what high quality accessible nightlife could be and nearly 15 years later the Bubble Club is now a house hold name to many in East London and an institution people with learning disabilities across the country have come to know and love.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Honest Reviews(5): We had the most amazing time at Go Ape and I can’t recommend it highly enough! From the moment we arrived, the whole experience was smooth, professional, and fun. The staff greeted us warmly and were super helpful throughout the entire visit. They took safety seriously, walking us through every detail in a clear and friendly way which definitely helped settle a few nerves in the group.  Once we got into the trees, the real adventure began. The course was fantastic a great mix of rope bridges, wobbly crossings, climbing elements, and of course the zip lines, which were the absolute highlight for everyone. Each section brought a new challenge and new laughs. It felt empowering to push through the tougher parts, especially knowing the harnesses and safety systems were rock-solid.  The scenery was beautiful too. Being up in the trees, completely surrounded by nature, made it feel like a mini escape from everyday life. It was peaceful and exhilarating all at once. You could feel the breeze up high, and then suddenly you’re flying down a zip line with the biggest smile on your face.  What really stood out was how well-run everything was. The team was always nearby in case you needed help, but they also gave you the freedom to go at your own pace. Whether you’re a thrill seeker or someone just trying to get out of your comfort zone, Go Ape strikes the perfect balance of excitement and safety. It’s also a fantastic workout without even realizing it!  I went with my partner, but it is also a great outing for families and friends, or even solo as you’ll be in a group with random people who you could make friends with. I know we definitely did. Everyone came away talking about how much fun they had.  In short, Go Ape delivered an unforgettable experience, challenging, exciting, and just pure fun from start to finish. If you’re even slightly considering it just go for it. You won’t regret it! | Jo Webber(5): Superb course, Florian was an accomplished instructor and we loved all the sites, which seemed to increase in difficulty as we progressed through the courses. The instructions were thorough, a good site to be more autonomous and go at the pace your group feels comfortable with. Highly recommended. | Jane Cubbidge(5): Great time at Go Ape Cockfosters with my 12 year old son. Florian was a superb instructor & after the tuition we both felt confident to take on the treetop challenge course. The course itself was very good, 5 separate courses each ending on a zip wire & each getting harder & higher up into the treetops!
gmaps_editorial_summary: Adventure-activity chain venue where groups explore the forest canopy via a treetop rope course.

They offer 1hr woodland rides, rides to the gallops (2hrs) to Trent Park, group lessons, private lessons and an opportunity to go to shows. They have a large indoor arena and a smaller outdoor one. Not ideal with an 18 month old. A little more organisation would go a long way. The trains go quite far through nice woodland and gardens. name:
Therapeutic choir- Hackney 
 Inclusive Notes
description_full:
Inclusive notes is a therapeutic choir ran by an occupational therapist, music specialist and medical doctor. Meet the team here. Through the team’s love of music and experience of working with children with additional needs this project has been born.
Inclusive notes is the first of its kind to use singing in a choir to help children develop their skills and achieve their therapeutic goals. Children will have fun, enjoy making music and succeed in a safe, accepting and engaging environment.

gmaps_reviews_top3: Keith Carrington(5): The museum itself is free! Though there is a cost for parking, plenty of room when I went. There are 6 hangars with aircraft from the dawn of aviation through to a mockup of an F35. There are information boards about each aircraft plus other static displays. There is a childrens play area outside and a cafe, plus of course a shop. Some air museums have rope barriers that are well away from the aircraft, but not here, you can get as close as safety and conservation allow, so lots of closeup photo opportunities. Some aircraft have steps next to them, plus some hangars have raised viewing areas. You can walk through a Sunderland flying boat. I did the Cold War experience, this is a charged extra - book well in advance. I sat in the Vulcan and Phantom and got inside the ropes with the Lightning, Vampire and several other aircraft on a one-to-one tour that was excellent, the guide was great. The tour was about 90 mins and proceeded at a good pace, it didn't feel rushed. In total I was in the museum for 4.5hrs, didn't stop for lunch, and took a large number of photos. Great day! | Bobby Dazzler(5): Oh. wow, you could spend literally days here and not see everything. You do need to get tickets prior to coming but to be honest, I didn't know that so I did it on line and it came through almost immediately and so it didn't impinge on me getting in at all. Certainly if your interested and like to read the entries. I am interested and I spent four hours, which was only scratching the surface. I had tea and cake which was excellent and did something I very rarely do, I bought some items from the shop, a Red Arrows cap for my grandson and an RAF tee-shirt, indulging myself. Considering this exhibition is free I can honestly say it is exceptional value for anyone, including families. The parking is fairly expensive by Yorkshire standards, but probably not by London. As you can read I was bowled over by the amount of exhibits which are grouped into buildings which form a historical timeline from the pre-inception days when it was a gentleman's hobby, into the first world war and the meteoric rise in the speed, manoeuvrability and firepower. Then on to the second world war, Spitfire, Hurricane, Lancaster and Halifax, to name but a few of the famous names. Then on through the Jet age into the present day, with the RAF's latest fighter/Bombers and missiles. Awesome, absolutely awesome and I will most definitely  be back to finish my tour and maybe just go round again because I will most certainly have missed loads. | Niel van Zyl(5): What a lovely museum! The stories are incredible and they have a large variety of aircraft from different countries. I could also really appreciate the engines that are shown next to most of the aircraft.  I would recommend it to anyone who loves planes, engine and war history.
gmaps_editorial_summary: Tracing the history of aviation & the RAF through real aircraft & interactive exhibits.

name: MahaDevi Yoga -Islington
description_full:
MahaDevi, Roberta Sumar Yoga Centre
12 Eden Grove, Stapleton House
http://mahadevicentre.com/special-needs/
Tel: 020 7609 0889
email: info@mahadevicentre.com
How Yoga helps
The first step in teaching Yoga to a student with autism is to establish a strong bond with the child. To do this the practitioner will need to enter the world that the child lives in - to meet the child on his or her own level, so to speak. Only then will the teacher be able to gain the child’s complete confidence. Massage, music, dance, rhymes and stories are some of the different techniques that the teacher can use to connect with the child.
As student and teacher gradually develop a foundation of mutual trust and friendship, the practitioner can introduce some of the Yoga poses (asanas) and breathing exercises (pranayama) that will help to bring the child with autism out of his or her shell and into the world of social interaction. After the student becomes familiar with these introductory poses, the practitioner may progressively add more asanas to the routine, as well as deep relaxation. The combination of asanas, pranayama and deep relaxation will strengthen the child’s nervous system, increase overall health and facilitate the development of body awareness and concentration. By establishing optimal physiological and psychological integrity, Yoga therapy helps children with autism gain new motor, communication and social skills. The end result is an overall improvement in their quality of life.
